Training covers days 0–120 (70%, ~35M impressions), validation covers days 121–150 (15%, ~7.5M impressions), and test covers days 151–180 (15%, ~7.5M impressions). No session or user appears in multiple splits. This temporal structure reflects the operational deployment scenario and prevents temporal leakage — the model cannot access future user behavior or emerging popularity trends unseen during training. A secondary stratification check confirms that query intent, result category, price range, and CTR distributions are stable across splits (KL divergence < 0.05); large shifts would signal dataset artifacts.

**3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing**
All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training set and applied to validation and test sets identically. This includes query tokenization (OOV tokens → UNK), numerical scaling of price and dwell time (fit mean/std on training), categorical encoding (unseen categories → rare-token), and session sequence length (95th percentile of training set distribution, with padding/truncation applied uniformly).

The transformer model uses: query token embeddings + session context (preceding 5–10 clicked items encoded via a separate transformer encoder) + product features (title, category, log-price, seller rating, popularity). The baseline uses only query embeddings, title embeddings, category, price, seller rating, and popularity — no session context.

**4. Model and Baseline**
The transformer ranker combines a pretrained BERT encoder (tuned on training data) with a dedicated session encoder (4 layers, 8 heads) and is trained end-to-end using pairwise ranking loss (or smoothed listwise rank loss) optimized for nDCG. The baseline is a gradient boosted decision tree ranker trained on identical preprocessed features (excluding session context) and tuned with equivalent effort — a full hyperparameter sweep over tree depth, learning rate, and regularization using validation-set nDCG@10. Both are trained on IPW-corrected click labels; both are tuned on validation data. This ensures a fair comparison that isolates the value of session-level features, not architectural differences or data quantity.

**5. Validation and Model Selection**
Hyperparameter tuning and model selection occur exclusively on the validation set (days 121–150). The test set is held and never touched until final evaluation. Early stopping is performed on validation nDCG@10. The hyperparameter configuration (for both models) that achieves the highest validation nDCG@10 is selected for test evaluation.

**6. Evaluation Metrics**
The primary metric is nDCG@10 on the held-out test set, computed using human-judged relevance labels (0–4 graded scale). nDCG directly reflects the hypothesis and stakeholder objective: users explore primarily the top 10 results, and better ranking quality in this window drives higher CTR and conversion. Significance is established via a two-tailed paired t-test on per-query nDCG scores.

Secondary metrics include: nDCG@5 (smaller consideration set), MRR (position of first relevant item), Recall@10, CTR on test impression traffic (if an online A/B test is conducted), purchase conversion rate within 7 days, nDCG@10 on de-biased (human-judged only) labels, and nDCG@10 on tail queries (low-frequency) to verify the gain generalizes to ambiguous searches.

**7. Confound Controls**
Position bias is controlled via IPW during training; the secondary metric (human labels) is free of position bias, isolating true ranking quality. Temporal confounds are prevented by chronological split; user/session composition is verified via stratification checks.

**8. Claims and Scope**
This design will establish whether session-level click sequences, when encoded via a transformer and combined with text relevance and popularity signals, improve top-10 ranking quality (nDCG@10) over a strong text+popularity baseline. It will not establish causation (correlation vs. causation requires online experimentation) or generalization to other domains. An online A/B test in production is necessary to confirm the offline gains translate to user-facing improvements and business metrics.
difficulty_justification: This case requires knowledge of IR metric design to distinguish MRR's focus on first-result recall from nDCG@10's sensitivity to full top-10 ranking quality, while avoiding pattern-matching biases against non-default metrics.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, a transformer model that incorporates raw alert sequences and host-context event graphs will rank true intrusion incidents higher than a baseline gradient-boosted model using handcrafted alert counts and severity features, as measured by top-k precision on future alerts.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
To test whether a transformer model leveraging raw alert sequences and host-context graphs outranks a handcrafted-feature baseline in enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, we propose the following experiment design.

**1. Data and Labeling**

We would use 24 months of production SOC alert data from a single organization or managed security provider. The dataset includes alert timestamps, rule IDs, host/user identifiers, severity scores, and analyst disposition labels linked to incident tickets. To ensure label quality and minimize asymmetric labeling bias, we retain only alerts that received an explicit analyst disposition (confirmed intrusion, benign, or false positive) within 30 days of alert generation. Alerts without a disposition are excluded. This yields a dataset of several million labeled alerts, typically with 0.5–2% positive rate (true incidents).

**2. Train-Validation-Test Split**

The 24-month window is split chronologically: months 1–16 for training (~67%), months 17–20 for validation (~17%), and months 21–24 for held-out testing (~17%). Critically, all alerts belonging to a single confirmed incident (identified by analyst ticket ID) are kept in the same fold, even if they span multiple days. This prevents leakage of contextual information from future alerts in the same incident into the training set. The chronological structure respects the deployment scenario: the model must rank alerts it has never seen before, using only historical patterns.

**3. Features and Preprocessing**

The transformer model receives Stream 1: raw alert sequences ordered by timestamp, with each alert tokenized by rule ID and augmented with metadata embeddings (host ID, user ID, severity, source IP subnet). Sequences are truncated or padded to 512 alerts per host per 24-hour window. In addition to per-alert metadata, each host sequence includes a rolling host state summary refreshed daily from the SOC telemetry pipeline, capturing recent containment-related activity such as quarantine status transitions and endpoint isolation flags. This summary is already available in the operational feature store and provides compact context about host criticality and response state without requiring separate feature engineering. All tokenization and embedding vocabularies are fit on training data only and frozen before validation.

The baseline gradient-boosted model receives Stream 2: handcrafted aggregates including alert count per rule (24h, 7d, 30d windows), alert severity histogram, unique hosts/users per rule, inter-alert time statistics, the same host state summary, and a host-risk score derived only from training data. Both models use the same training set and ranking loss (LambdaMART), ensuring equivalent learning opportunity. The baseline is not given access to raw sequences; this design isolates the value of learned sequence representation.

**4. Model Selection and Validation**

Hyperparameters for both the transformer and baseline are tuned via grid search on the validation set. The combination maximizing MAP@10 on validation data is selected. The test set is held out and touched only once, after selection is complete.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**

The primary metric is Mean Average Precision at k=10 (MAP@10). For each 24-hour period in the test set, we compute average precision of the top 10 ranked alerts. This directly reflects the stakeholder objective: analysts review only a small number of alerts per shift, and high precision at the top ensures analyst effort focuses on genuine incidents. Secondary metrics include NDCG@10 (ranking quality), Precision@10 and Recall@10 (precision/recall trade-off), Top-1 Hit Rate (best alert is true incident), and calibration (decile analysis of score vs. empirical incident rate).

**6. Confound Controls**

We control for three major confounds. First, temporal leakage is prevented by chronological splits and co-incident clustering. Second, information asymmetry is eliminated by ensuring the baseline has access to equivalent host and rule context (as aggregated features). Third, labeling delay is controlled by requiring all retained labels to be assigned within 30 days. As a robustness check, we report test performance separately for recent (first 7 days of each month) vs. older alerts (last 7 days) to quantify residual labeling bias. Any substantial performance gap between recent and older alerts would indicate that ground truth is incomplete for recent alerts and would limit the strength of conclusions.

**7. Scope**

This experiment establishes whether, on the test period and labeled alerts from a single organization, the transformer model achieves higher ranking precision than the baseline. It does not establish whether the transformer generalizes to other organizations, other SIEM platforms, or alert rule sets not seen during training. Separate generalization studies would be required to assess those claims.

planted_issues.description: The rolling host state summary captures containment-related updates (e.g., quarantine status transitions, endpoint isolation) that are part of analyst incident response actions. Since these updates often occur after the original alert is triaged, including them in the feature set leaks information about downstream incident resolution, allowing the model to exploit proxy signals for the true label rather than genuine sequence patterns.

must_not_claim.claim: The host-risk score derived from training data introduces leakage into the baseline model.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The host-risk score is explicitly derived from training data alone and reflects static properties of hosts (e.g., historical quarantine status) rather than future labels. This aligns with the need to control for information asymmetry by ensuring the baseline has access to equivalent context about host criticality, which is a legitimate design choice.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates that the host state summary includes time-dependent containment actions that are causally linked to analyst confirmation of incidents. The flaw invalidates comparisons by allowing both models to exploit signals that would not be available at the true triage decision time.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Analysis shows that containment-related host state changes in the feature set coincide with alerts that later received analyst confirmation, creating a feedback loop where response actions leak into features used for prediction.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The defense argues that the host state summary reflects pre-response telemetry rather than incident resolution signals. The critic rebuts by showing empirical correlations between host state flags and label positivity in the test set, indicating unresolved data leakage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Statistical analysis of test alerts reveals significant overlap between host state summary flags and confirmed intrusion labels, suggesting the features encode downstream confirmation signals.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The host state summary is proven to capture only pre-confirmation telemetry (e.g., hardware health metrics, baseline network activity) unrelated to incident response actions, validated through audit logs.

hypothesis: A transformer-based retrieval model that uses full clinical question context and recent longitudinal lab trends will outperform a bag-of-words BM25 baseline for matching oncology clinicians to the most relevant biomedical abstracts, measured by top-10 relevance and downstream answer usefulness.
domain: Clinical decision support for oncology literature search in a hospital setting
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Retrieval for Oncology Literature Search

### 1. Data Collection and Curation
The experiment uses 18 months of production logs from a single large teaching hospital's oncology knowledge-search system, capturing timestamped clinician queries, clicked abstracts, dwell time, session identifiers (linking multiple queries within a single patient encounter), and follow-up actions (saved citations, full-text opens). The indexed corpus comprises ~50,000 unique PubMed abstracts relevant to oncology, with full metadata (title, authors, MeSH terms, publication date). This single-site approach avoids cross-hospital distribution shift and keeps the confound space tractable.

### 2. Label Acquisition and Treatment of Position Bias
Implicit labels are defined conservatively: a click within 30 seconds of query submission and dwell time ≥15 seconds is marked positive. This threshold reduces noise from accidental clicks and interface browsing. However, click data is heavily influenced by ranking position and interface design—a document ranked first will be clicked more often regardless of relevance. To obtain unbiased labels, a stratified random sample of 2,000 queries (sampled to represent the distribution of cancer types and query lengths) is sent for explicit relevance judgment by three independent oncology clinicians on a 3-point scale (not relevant / somewhat relevant / highly relevant). Disagreement is resolved by majority vote. Strong explicit labels (≥2 votes for 'highly relevant') form the gold standard for test evaluation; weak implicit labels are used for training only. This separation decouples model training (where weak labels are acceptable) from evaluation (where strong labels are required).

### 3. Split Strategy: Session-Level Stratified Partitioning
To prevent related interactions from appearing in multiple partitions while maintaining comparable case mix, the data is split at the clinical-session level. All queries are grouped by clinical session (clinician ID + date); sessions are the atomic unit, not individual queries. Sessions are then assigned to training, validation, and test in a 60/20/20 proportion while preserving the joint distribution of cancer type (top 8 types representing ~80% of queries), query length quartile, and calendar month across splits. Matching the month-of-year composition reduces the chance that seasonal oncology workload or shifts in the indexed literature dominate the comparison. Because the unit of assignment is the full session, related queries within a single patient encounter remain contained within one split, preventing the model from benefiting from near-duplicate context across partitions.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
For the transformer model, raw inputs are: query text (with [SPECIALTY] tokens prepended to indicate the clinician's specialty), document title, abstract text, MeSH terms (as [MESH_term] tokens), and recency (days since publication, binned into 5 quantile buckets). Recent longitudinal lab trends are captured as follows: if a query contains numeric values or lab test names (detected via regex and LOINC code matching), the model appends the most recent 6 months of prior queries by the same clinician for the same patient session and extracts lab-related terms as context tokens. A shared transformer encoder (SciBERT or PubMedBERT, 768-dim) encodes queries as [CLS] query_tokens [SPECIALTY] [lab_context] [SEP] and documents as [CLS] title [SEP] abstract [SEP] mesh_terms [SEP]. All preprocessing transformers (tokenizer vocabulary, MeSH taxonomy) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets, preventing data leakage.

For the BM25 baseline, the same query text and document (title + abstract) are used, but MeSH terms and recency features are excluded. This ensures a fair comparison: any performance gap reflects semantic matching capability, not feature engineering.

### 5. Model Architecture and Training
The transformer model uses a contrastive learning objective (InfoNCE loss, temperature 0.07) with in-batch negatives. Hard negatives are mined from the BM25 baseline applied to the training set: for each query, the top-20 BM25 results (excluding the clicked document) serve as negatives. This mining strategy ensures the model learns to disambiguate between plausible but incorrect results, not just separate queries from random documents. Training is performed over 3 epochs with learning rate scheduling (warmup 1,000 steps, linear decay).

### 6. Baseline and Fair Comparison
BM25 (Okapi BM25, k1=1.5, b=0.75 by default) is the baseline. Its hyperparameters are tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same ranking metrics, ensuring equivalent tuning effort. Both models are evaluated on the same test set using identical ranking protocols.

### 7. Evaluation and Model Selection
Hyperparameter tuning occurs via 10-fold cross-validation within the training set. The validation set selects the final model: the checkpoint with the highest MRR@10 on the validation set (against weak labels) is chosen. Crucially, the test set is held entirely separate and used only once, at the end, to compute final metrics against the explicit strong labels. This one-time-only rule prevents adaptive overfitting to the test set.

### 8. Primary and Secondary Metrics
The primary metric is **MRR@10** (mean reciprocal rank at 10), computed on the 2,000 explicitly annotated queries. MRR@10 is chosen because the central question in this workflow is whether the system surfaces a clearly useful paper immediately; clinicians often benefit most from the first strong hit rather than incremental improvements deeper in the ranked list. The metric therefore emphasizes time-to-useful-evidence in the top portion of the ranking while remaining easy to interpret at the query level. Secondary metrics include nDCG@10 (to retain graded relevance analysis), recall@10 (coverage), and precision@10 (irrelevant article burden).

### 9. Confound Controls
Several sensitivity analyses are pre-specified: (1) **Query length stratification**: MRR@10 is reported separately for short, medium, and long queries; uniform gains across groups suggest the advantage is not merely exploitation of query sophistication. (2) **Cancer type breakdown**: MRR@10 is reported per major cancer type to detect specialty-specific bias. (3) **Popularity bias check**: compare performance on frequently clicked vs. rarely clicked articles; overfitting to implicit bias would show degraded performance on low-popularity documents. (4) **Lab context ablation**: train a transformer variant without lab trends; identical performance would indicate this feature does not contribute. (5) **Implicit-explicit label correlation**: compute Spearman correlation between click-based rankings and explicit judgments; low correlation validates the need for explicit evaluation.

### 10. Scope and Limitations
This design establishes whether the transformer model outperforms BM25 on MRR@10 within this single hospital's oncology domain, using explicit relevance labels. It does **not** establish whether improved rankings translate to faster clinical decisions or better patient outcomes (that would require a downstream prospective clinical trial). It does **not** generalize to other hospitals, specialties, or query types without explicit validation. The design controls for query length, cancer type, and popularity bias but cannot rule out all confounds (e.g., document recency effects, author prestige).

planted_issues.description: The split strategy balances calendar month across splits but does not sort sessions chronologically. This allows future interactions to influence model training and evaluation, invalidating the prospective simulation of real-world deployment. Document recency features and lab trends derived from prior queries in the session will have access to future data, creating artificial performance gains.

Using MRR@10 as primary metric discards relevant information about lower-ranked but useful documents and conflates 'somewhat relevant' and 'highly relevant' labels. This contradicts the hypothesis's emphasis on top-10 relevance coverage and graded clinical relevance, favoring models that rank a single strong hit over those with multiple useful documents in the top results.

must_not_claim.claim: The session-level stratification introduces unnecessary complexity and could lead to imbalanced cancer type distribution
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Session-level stratification is essential to prevent leakage while maintaining representative case mix. The method explicitly preserves cancer type and query length distributions across splits as required by the domain.

must_not_claim.claim: Using MRR@10 simplifies interpretation by focusing on the most relevant document
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis emphasizes top-10 relevance for clinical decisioning, where multiple supporting documents often matter. MRR@10 ignores graded relevance distinctions and mid-list useful documents, misaligning with the operational use case.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates temporal leakage through (1) analysis of recency feature distributions across splits showing future bias, or (2) ablation experiments where models overfit to month-predictive patterns. The verdict requires acknowledging invalidation of prospective evaluation claims if leakage is found.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Evidence of systematic correlation between session dates and split assignments, or performance gains on time-sensitive documents in test set

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic shows that MRR@10 leads to incorrect model comparisons by analyzing secondary metrics (e.g., nDCG@10) where the model underperforms despite high MRR@10. The verdict requires switching primary metric back to nDCG@10 if validated.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Significant gains in MRR@10 without corresponding improvement in nDCG@10 or coverage metrics, indicating prioritization of first-hit success over comprehensive relevance
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Empirical demonstration that clinicians almost exclusively rely on the top-ranked document in practice, validated via clickstream analysis

## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Retrieval for Oncology Literature Search

### 1. Data Collection and Curation
[...]

### 3. Split Strategy: Session-Stratified Temporal Division
To prevent two distinct forms of leakage, the data is split as follows. First, all queries are grouped by clinical session (clinician ID + date); sessions are the atomic unit, not individual queries. Sessions are sorted chronologically and divided: 60% to training, 20% to validation, 20% to test. This temporal ordering ensures no future queries influence past model parameters, preventing temporal leakage. Second, within each split, sessions are stratified by cancer type (top 8 types representing ~80% of queries) and query length quartile, ensuring representative coverage of the oncology domain's diversity. This session-level split also prevents leakage of related queries within a single patient encounter, which would otherwise allow the model to cheat by memorizing query patterns within a session.

### 8. Primary and Secondary Metrics
The primary metric is **nDCG@10** (normalized discounted cumulative gain at rank 10), computed on the 2,000 explicitly annotated queries. nDCG@10 is chosen because (1) clinicians scan the top 10 results in practice, (2) it penalizes low-relevance items at the top (precision where it matters), and (3) it accounts for graded relevance, reflecting how clinicians actually evaluate evidence.
difficulty_justification: Temporal leakage is disguised through careful-seeming month-balancing, and MRR@10 appears justifiable for "first hit" use cases, requiring domain-specific knowledge to detect misalignments with the hypothesis and clinical workflow.

hypothesis: For hospital-acquired sepsis prediction from EHR data, a temporally aware sequence model trained on hourly vitals, labs, and medication administrations will predict onset within the next 6 hours more accurately than a traditional feature-engineered logistic regression baseline using aggregated last-observation-carried-forward features.
domain: Acute inpatient care in a large hospital network using electronic health records for ward and ICU patients
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporally Aware Sequence Models for Sepsis Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

This experiment uses retrospective EHR data spanning 36 months (January 2021 through December 2023) from a large hospital network. The cohort includes all adult ward and ICU admissions with at least 6 hours of continuous monitoring. For each patient, we extract timestamped measurements of vitals (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, temperature, oxygen saturation, FiO2), laboratory values (WBC, platelets, lactate, creatinine, bilirubin, PT, procalcitonin), medication administrations (antibiotics, vasopressors, fluids, sedatives), and transfer events. We exclude neonatal ICU patients, patients under 18 years old, admissions with fewer than 6 hours of data, and transfers from other hospitals with unknown baseline state, as these lack sufficient signal for reliable prediction.

Sepsis labels are derived using clinical criteria that combine objective markers: hospital-acquired sepsis is defined as the earlier of (1) blood culture order AND antibiotic start within ±12 hours, (2) sepsis diagnosis code (ICD-10 R65.1x) with concurrent organ dysfunction (acute kidney injury, ARDS, thrombocytopenia, vasopressor-requiring hypotension), or (3) qSOFA ≥2 plus organ dysfunction on the same day. Sepsis must occur >48 hours post-admission to isolate hospital-acquired cases. The label timestamp is the earliest of these clinical markers. Admissions that reach 30 days post-admission or discharge without meeting any criterion are labeled non-sepsis. Cases with uncertain or suspected but unconfirmed sepsis are excluded to minimize label noise.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally by admission date to prevent information leakage and respect the causal timeline. Training admissions span January 2021 through June 2022 (18 months), validation spans July 2022 through December 2022 (6 months), and test spans January 2023 through December 2023 (12 months). The 6-month gaps between splits allow seasonal variation and concept drift to be captured. Within each split, admissions are stratified by hospital site and sepsis prevalence quartile to ensure no single site or prevalence pattern dominates any fold. This temporal split is essential because in real deployment, the model sees only past data; using random splits would artificially allow the model to learn from post-onset measurements that would not be available at prediction time.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

The sequence model operates on hourly timesteps. Data are resampled to hourly resolution: multiple measurements within one hour are aggregated (median for vitals, most recent for labs). Missing values are forward-filled up to 24 hours with a binary missingness indicator appended; values missing >24 hours are treated as unknown and flagged. Each timestep contains 20 dimensions: 6 vitals, 7 labs, 4 medication flags (antibiotic, vasopressor, fluid bolus, sedative), and 3 census features (hours since transfer, ICU status). Sequences are truncated or padded to 168 hours (7 days).

For the logistic regression baseline, features are hand-engineered using a 24-hour sliding window: last-observation-carried-forward vitals (6), max/min/mean statistics for labs in the window (21), medication flags (4), rate of change for lactate, WBC, and creatinine (3), and interaction terms (lactate×HR, WBC×fever) (2), totaling 36 features. All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation parameters) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage from validation/test distributions.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The primary model is a 2-layer bidirectional LSTM with attention. Architecture: 128 hidden units per direction per layer, attention pooling, then dense layers (64, 32 units with ReLU activation), and a sigmoid output. Hyperparameters are tuned via grid search on the validation set: learning rate ∈ {1e-3, 5e-4, 1e-4}, dropout ∈ {0.2, 0.3, 0.4}, attention heads ∈ {4, 8}, batch size ∈ {32, 64}. Early stopping uses validation AUC-ROC with patience of 10 epochs.

The baseline is logistic regression trained on the 36 aggregated features described above. It receives equal tuning effort: grid search over regularization C ∈ {0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100} and class weight adjustment. Both models are evaluated on identical train/val/test splits and the same primary metric. This ensures the comparison isolates the benefit of temporal structure and learned interactions, not better feature engineering or hyperparameter search.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is patient-level Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC-ROC) at a 6-hour prediction horizon. For each admission, hourly predictions are generated from 6 to 48 hours post-admission and then reduced to a single summary score using the maximum predicted risk observed in that interval. Admissions are treated as positive if hospital-acquired sepsis occurs after the 48-hour post-admission threshold and negative otherwise. This formulation is intended to assess whether the model successfully identifies admissions that ever become septic while reducing sensitivity to timestamp noise in retrospective charting, where the exact ordering of culture orders, antibiotic starts, and documentation can vary by several hours.

Secondary metrics include sensitivity at 90% specificity (the true positive rate while constraining false alerts to 10%), positive predictive value (the fraction of alerts that precede sepsis), time-to-alert (median hours before sepsis onset when the model first triggers), and calibration (Brier score and calibration plots). These contextualize the model's clinical utility and confidence calibration.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameter tuning is performed exclusively on the validation set. For each candidate hyperparameter combination, the model is trained on the training set and patient-level AUC-ROC is evaluated on the validation set. The best hyperparameter set is locked before any test-set evaluation. The test set is held out and touched exactly once, at the end, to report final performance. All reported metrics include 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling of test-set predictions.

### 7. Confound Control

Label leakage around transfer and ICU events is mitigated by (1) excluding predictions made within 2 hours of any ward/ICU transfer, as such events often trigger documentation reviews that can retroactively alter timestamps, (2) auditing the label pipeline to verify antibiotic start times and culture orders are clinically plausible, and (3) running a sensitivity analysis with a stricter label definition (blood culture + antibiotic concordance within 6 hours). Missingness patterns are a potential confounder because high missingness in ICU could proxy for severity independent of the sequence model's temporal structure; this is controlled by fitting imputation parameters on training data, ablating the missingness indicator feature, and stratifying test-set performance by ICU vs. ward status. External validity is assessed by comparing demographics, sepsis prevalence, and site distribution across splits and reporting stratified performance by site.

planted_issues.description: The primary metric evaluates patient-level AUC-ROC using maximum predicted risk across the entire risk window instead of horizon-specific forecasting. This obscures whether the sequence model provides earlier actionable warnings within the 6-hour lead time required by the hypothesis, conflating early detection with general sepsis risk stratification.

must_not_claim.claim: The sequence model's performance improvement could stem from better handling of missing values or ICU-specific patterns rather than temporal structure learning.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The experiment explicitly controls for missingness-related confounding through training-data-fitted imputation, missingness indicator ablation studies, and stratified ICU/ward performance analysis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic demonstrates that patient-level metric evaluation fails to validate the hypothesis's claim about <6-hour early warning, and the defense concedes this limitation while proposing horizon-specific metrics for follow-up analysis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Analysis shows temporal models achieve similar or inferior early warning lead time compared to baselines despite better overall patient stratification.

hypothesis: A transformer encoder trained on raw claim line-item sequences and provider billing codes will predict next-90-day high-cost utilization better than a gradient-boosted model built from aggregated member-level claims features for commercial health insurance members.

task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Encoder for Healthcare Claims-Based High-Cost Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort Definition

This experiment uses 18 months of de-identified medical and pharmacy claims from a single commercial payer. The cohort consists of members continuously enrolled for at least 6 months prior to the observation window, with at least 2 prior claims in the lookback period, and excludes members with fixed-cost diagnoses (e.g., end-stage renal disease) where utilization is clinically determined rather than discretionary. Expected cohort size: 200k–500k members. Claims are extracted as line-item records including diagnosis codes (ICD-10), procedure codes (CPT/HCPCS), drug codes (NDC), allowed amounts, dates of service, and provider identifiers.

### 2. Label Construction

The label is a binary indicator of high-intensity utilization in the subsequent 90 days. Specifically, for each member at the close of an observation window, we determine whether that member enters the top utilization decile in the following 90-day period, where utilization is defined as total claim line count across medical and pharmacy claims. This formulation captures members likely to generate concentrated near-term care demand while avoiding sensitivity to payer-specific reimbursement schedules and negotiated rate variation in allowed amounts. The label is assigned deterministically and is never updated retroactively.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy

To prevent leakage of future care patterns and to mirror the prospective deployment scenario, the experiment uses a strict temporal split with non-overlapping cohorts:
- **Training cohort** (months 1–8): Members with 6-month claim histories and labels assigned from follow-up months 7–9.
- **Validation cohort** (months 9–13): Members with 6-month claim histories and labels from follow-up months 11–13. Hyperparameter tuning is performed on this set.
- **Test cohort** (months 14–18): Members with 6-month claim histories and labels from follow-up months 16–18. This set is held out until final evaluation.

No member ID appears across splits. This design prevents information leakage: the model cannot observe a claim from the label window during training. Temporal splits are essential because (a) claims are time-ordered and a future claim's presence could indirectly inform predictions on the past, (b) healthcare trends and seasonality require temporal structure, and (c) deployment is prospective: the model scores a static historical window and waits to observe outcomes.

### 4. Feature Engineering

Two parallel feature engineering pipelines are constructed from the same 6-month lookback:

**Transformer input (raw sequence):** Claim line items are ordered by date of service and represented as tuples: (diagnosis code, procedure code, drug code, allowed amount, days offset from window start). Sequences are padded/truncated to 512 tokens. Codes are embedded via learned vocabularies (trained on training set only). Amounts and offsets are standardized using training set statistics. No information from the label window (days 91–180) is included.

**Baseline features (gradient-boosted):** Engineered temporal summaries include aggregated costs (total, mean per claim), recency (days since last claim, rolling 30/60/90-day counts), diagnosis prevalence (one-hot encoding of top 50 ICD-10 codes), procedure diversity (distinct CPT counts), and pharmacy signals (NDC counts, therapeutic classes). Both feature sets include member age, sex, and geography.

All preprocessing transformers are fit on the training set only and applied consistently to validation and test sets. This ensures realistic evaluation under unknown future data distributions.

### 5. Model Architecture and Baseline

**Transformer model:** A 6-layer encoder with 8 attention heads, d_model=256, feedforward dimension=1024, and dropout=0.1. The [CLS] token embedding is passed through a 2-layer MLP (256→64→1 with sigmoid) to produce the probability of high-cost utilization.

**Baseline model:** Gradient-boosted decision trees (LightGBM or XGBoost) with 200 trees, max_depth=7, learning_rate=0.05. This represents current practice in claims-based risk modeling. Both the transformer and baseline receive equal tuning effort: random search or Bayesian optimization over 50–100 hyperparameter configurations, evaluated on the validation set.

### 6. Metrics and Model Selection

**Primary metric:** Precision at 10% recall. This directly reflects the operational constraint: stakeholders can outreach to ~10% of members per cycle. At fixed recall, precision quantifies how many flagged members are true high-cost cases; this aligns incentives to reduce false positives that waste resources. AUROC is avoided because it masks class imbalance (~10% positive rate) and ignores the fixed-capacity constraint.

**Secondary metrics:** Recall at precision ≥90%, precision-recall AUC, calibration (expected calibration error by decile), and stratified performance by prior-period cost decile.

Model selection uses the validation set (months 9–13). The checkpoint with the highest precision at 10% recall on validation is selected. Early stopping and hyperparameter search use a 20/80 split of the validation set to avoid overfitting to the full validation set.

### 7. Test Set Evaluation and Controls

The test set remains untouched during development. After model selection, both models are re-trained on combined training and validation data (months 1–13) using selected hyperparameters, then evaluated once on the test set. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (1000 resamples).

To address the confound that transformer gains may reflect sequence length or recency rather than richer clinical structure, the design includes:
- **Recency and length baselines:** The gradient-boosted baseline explicitly includes days-since-last-claim and rolling-count features, ensuring recency signal is not absent from the comparison.
- **Ablation:** Transformer trained with randomized claim order to verify gain is not attributable to self-attention itself.
- **Stratified analysis:** Performance is reported separately for short, medium, and long claim sequences; imbalanced gains favor the transformer on only long sequences would suggest recency capture rather than clinical insight.
- **Attention inspection:** Top-ranked attention heads are reviewed post-hoc to identify whether learned patterns correspond to clinically meaningful transitions.
- **Imbalance handling:** Focal loss and stratified splits preserve label prevalence across folds; the primary metric is inherently robust to imbalance.

planted_issues.description: The experiment redefines 'high-cost utilization' as high claim line count (volume), diverging from the hypothesis's focus on high allowed amounts (cost). Claim volume and cost can materially differ—a member might have few high-cost claims (e.g., inpatient admission) or many low-cost claims (e.g., prescriptions). This misalignment invalidates the hypothesis test, as the transformer's ability to capture cost drivers is not being evaluated.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of precision at 10% recall is inappropriate due to class imbalance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Precision at 10% recall is explicitly aligned with the stakeholder's fixed-capacity outreach constraint and is robust to the 10% positive rate in the label, directly addressing the hypothesis's operational requirements.

must_not_claim.claim: Temporal splits with non-overlapping cohorts are unnecessarily restrictive; the test set could be shorter.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits with non-overlapping members and windows (months 14–18) mirror prospective deployment and prevent leakage of future care patterns, which is critical for claims data with time-ordered dependencies.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic must establish that the label definition misaligns with the hypothesis's objective of predicting high-cost utilization. The final verdict should conclude that the corrupted design invalidates the study's premise.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that claim line count and allowed costs measure distinct aspects of utilization, and that the hypothesis specifically concerns predicting high-cost cases rather than high-volume activity.
