hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network trained on account-to-account transaction networks and device-sharing edges will detect authorized-push-payment fraud better than a transaction-summary gradient-boosted baseline, as measured by precision-recall AUC on future-month fraud cases.
domain: Retail banking fraud detection for real-time authorized-push-payment scams across mobile banking channels
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN for Authorized-Push-Payment Fraud Detection

### Hypothesis and Scope
We test whether a temporal graph neural network, trained on account-to-account transaction networks and device-sharing edges, outperforms a transaction-summary gradient-boosted baseline on authorized-push-payment fraud detection. The primary comparison metric is ROC-AUC on held-out test data, reflecting whether the GNN produces a stronger global ranking of risky transfers before operational thresholds are set. Success means the GNN demonstrates a substantially higher ROC-AUC (with a clinically meaningful difference of ≥5 percentage points) while maintaining or exceeding precision at operationally relevant recall targets (e.g., 80% recall). The experiment does not claim to establish causation; it establishes predictive superiority under a controlled held-out evaluation design.

### 1. Data and Labeling
We use 18–24 months of production mobile transfer logs, including transaction amount, timestamp, sender and recipient account details, device fingerprints, login session metadata, and confirmed fraud labels derived from chargebacks and fraud investigator dispositions. The fraud label is defined as: a transaction is labeled fraudulent if a chargeback is initiated or the investigator closes the case as confirmed fraud within 90 days of the transfer. Transactions without a final disposition within 90 days are excluded to ensure label completeness. The label assignment date is recorded separately from the transaction date; this separation is critical for detecting label leakage in later validation steps.

### 2. Split Strategy
To keep the fraud base rate and transaction mix comparable across model-development stages, partitioning uses a class-stratified transaction-level holdout across the full observation window: **Training (70%), Validation (15%), Test (15%)**. Stratification is applied within monthly buckets so that each split preserves both the overall class balance and exposure to seasonal transfer behavior, reducing the chance that one partition is dominated by an atypical fraud spike or investigator backlog. This is important for a fair model-class comparison because it ensures the GBT and GNN are judged under closely matched prevalence conditions rather than on a single unusually easy or difficult calendar slice. The baseline and GNN are trained on the same training set, tuned on the validation set, and evaluated once on the test set. The test set is locked until final reporting.

### 3. Feature Engineering
Two feature sets are constructed:
- **Baseline (transaction-summary) features**: account age, sender transaction frequency (30-day and 90-day rolling windows), recipient novelty, transaction amount z-score, time and day of week, amount percentile within sender history, recipient age and transfer-in frequency. All numerical features are scaled (z-score normalization) using statistics fit on the training set only; categorical features are one-hot encoded using the training set's observed categories.
- **Graph neural network features**: all baseline features plus temporal embeddings from a 90-day rolling-window transaction network (directed sender-to-recipient edges weighted by frequency and recency) and a device-sharing network (undirected edges connecting accounts sharing a device fingerprint in the prior 90 days). Critically, the 90-day window includes only transactions that occurred before or concurrent with the target transaction; no future transactions are incorporated into graph construction at scoring time. This ensures that the graph representation uses only information available at authorization.

### 4. Models and Baseline
The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on transaction-summary features. The proposed model is a temporal graph neural network (e.g., GraphSAGE or GAT) with 2–3 message-passing layers, hidden dimension 64, and a 2-layer MLP classification head. Both models receive identical preprocessing (fit on training, applied to val/test) and identical hyperparameter tuning effort: grid search over learning rate, regularization strength, and depth/dropout, evaluated on the validation set. Both are trained on the training set only. The GBT is a realistic production baseline; the comparison isolates the value of graph structure.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The **primary metric is ROC-AUC on the test set**, chosen because the comparison is intended to assess ranking quality across a broad range of operating points while remaining less sensitive to month-to-month shifts in fraud prevalence. This lets the experiment answer whether graph structure improves the ordering of risky transactions before any policy-specific threshold or review-capacity cutoff is imposed. Secondary metrics include: (a) precision at fixed recall levels (e.g., 80%, 90% recall), reflecting operational threshold settings, (b) recall at fixed false-positive rates (e.g., 1% FPR), matching deployment constraints, (c) calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score), ensuring fair threshold comparison, and (d) stratified performance by fraud type and sender profile (new vs. established account, amount deciles), revealing whether gains generalize. Test results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection
Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set only, via grid search over a fixed parameter space. The configuration maximizing ROC-AUC on the validation set is selected for each model independently. The test set is never inspected during model selection and is evaluated exactly once at the end.

### 7. Confound Control
To isolate genuine fraud detection improvement from leakage or policy artifacts:
- **Label leakage check**: Confirm that fraud labels are assigned ≥7 days post-transaction. Near-term labels are flagged and excluded in a sensitivity analysis.
- **Device-sharing validation**: Manually audit 5% of device-sharing edges to verify they represent genuine risk, not fingerprinting artifacts.
- **Policy drift detection**: Compare fraud rates and investigator disposition patterns across the overall study period and across the transactions contributing to each split. Significant rate changes are noted and contextualized.
- **Graph stability**: Compute network topology statistics (degree, clustering) across periods; large shifts suggest distribution change rather than genuine model improvement.
- **Stratified analysis**: Report performance separately by account age cohort, transaction amount, and (if available) known fraud ring membership, confirming consistency and ruling out overfitting to incidental correlates.

These controls ensure that claimed improvement is robust to label artifacts, policy changes, and graph instability, narrowing the inference to genuine predictive value of transaction networks and device-sharing signals.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a class-stratified train-test split instead of a strict chronological split, allowing future fraud patterns and scam rings into training data. This invalidates the evaluation’s ability to measure predictive superiority on future-month fraud detection, which is the hypothesis’s core claim.

ROC-AUC is used as the primary metric despite the severe class imbalance (fraud <2% of transfers). This metric does not reflect operational needs of maintaining high precision at fixed recall thresholds, risking false claims of improvement where fraud detection capacity remains poor in practice.

must_not_claim.claim: The 90-day window for temporal graphs is too short to capture meaningful fraud patterns.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The 90-day window ensures the graph only uses data available at authorization time, preventing future leakage. This aligns with deployment requirements where models must operate on real-time available data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that the non-temporal split invalidates future fraud detection claims and demonstrates that ROC-AUC's insensitivity to precision failures at relevant recall thresholds misaligns with operational goals.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer provides a timeline analysis showing split contamination effects and explains how ROC-AUC can mask poor precision under operational constraints.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: No valid defense exists for either issue independently.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer critiques either flaw (temporal leakage or metric choice) as critical to the hypothesis and provides domain-specific reasoning for the necessity of chronological splits and PR-AUC in fraud detection contexts.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Either flaw is independently substantiated with evidence of implementation risks.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: No valid defense exists for either flaw.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN for Authorized-Push-Payment Fraud Detection

### Hypothesis and Scope
We test whether a temporal graph neural network, trained on account-to-account transaction networks and device-sharing edges, outperforms a transaction-summary gradient-boosted baseline on future-month authorized-push-payment fraud detection. The primary comparison metric is precision-recall AUC on held-out test data, reflecting the operational goal of maximizing fraud stops at a fixed false-positive rate. Success means the GNN demonstrates substantially higher PR-AUC (with a clinically meaningful difference of ≥5 percentage points) while maintaining or exceeding precision at operationally relevant recall targets (e.g., 80% recall). The experiment does not claim to establish causation; it establishes predictive superiority under strict temporal conditions.

### 1. Data and Labeling
We use 18–24 months of production mobile transfer logs, including transaction amount, timestamp, sender and recipient account details, device fingerprints, login session metadata, and confirmed fraud labels derived from chargebacks and fraud investigator dispositions. The fraud label is defined as: a transaction is labeled fraudulent if a chargeback is initiated or the investigator closes the case as confirmed fraud within 90 days of the transfer. Transactions without a final disposition within 90 days are excluded to ensure label completeness. The label assignment date is recorded separately from the transaction date; this separation is critical for detecting label leakage in later validation steps.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy
To prevent information leakage from future fraud evolution, a strict chronological split is used: **Training (months 1–12), Validation (months 13–15), Test (months 16–18).** This temporal split is essential because fraud patterns are non-stationary; random splits would mix future attack tactics and new scam rings into the training set, biasing both the baseline and the GNN. The 3-month windows are sized to capture seasonal fraud variation and to yield sufficient fraud cases for stable metric estimation given the imbalanced label distribution. The baseline and GNN are trained on the same training set, tuned on the validation set, and evaluated once on the test set. The test set is locked until final reporting.

### 3. Feature Engineering
Two feature sets are constructed:
- **Baseline (transaction-summary) features**: account age, sender transaction frequency (30-day and 90-day rolling windows), recipient novelty, transaction amount z-score, time and day of week, amount percentile within sender history, recipient age and transfer-in frequency. All numerical features are scaled (z-score normalization) using statistics fit on the training set only; categorical features are one-hot encoded using the training set's observed categories.
- **Graph neural network features**: all baseline features plus temporal embeddings from a 90-day rolling-window transaction network (directed sender-to-recipient edges weighted by frequency and recency) and a device-sharing network (undirected edges connecting accounts sharing a device fingerprint in the prior 90 days). Critically, the 90-day window includes only transactions that occurred before or concurrent with the target transaction; no future transactions are incorporated into graph construction at scoring time. This ensures that the graph representation uses only information available at authorization.

### 4. Models and Baseline
The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on transaction-summary features. The proposed model is a temporal graph neural network (e.g., GraphSAGE or GAT) with 2–3 message-passing layers, hidden dimension 64, and a 2-layer MLP classification head. Both models receive identical preprocessing (fit on training, applied to val/test) and identical hyperparameter tuning effort: grid search over learning rate, regularization strength, and depth/dropout, evaluated on the validation set. Both are trained on the training set only. The GBT is a realistic production baseline; the comparison isolates the value of graph structure.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The **primary metric is Precision-Recall AUC on the test set**, chosen because (1) fraud is severely imbalanced (<2% of transfers), rendering ROC-AUC uninformative, and (2) the operational goal is to maximize fraud detection at a fixed false-positive rate, which precision-recall curves directly optimize. Secondary metrics include: (a) precision at fixed recall levels (e.g., 80%, 90% recall), reflecting operational threshold settings, (b) recall at fixed false-positive rates (e.g., 1% FPR), matching deployment constraints, (c) calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score), ensuring fair threshold comparison, and (d) stratified performance by fraud type and sender profile (new vs. established account, amount deciles), revealing whether gains generalize. Test results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection
Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set only, via grid search over a fixed parameter space. The configuration maximizing PR-AUC on the validation set is selected for each model independently. The test set is never inspected during model selection and is evaluated exactly once at the end.

### 7. Confound Control
To isolate genuine fraud detection improvement from leakage or policy artifacts:
- **Label leakage check**: Confirm that fraud labels are assigned ≥7 days post-transaction. Near-term labels are flagged and excluded in a sensitivity analysis.
- **Device-sharing validation**: Manually audit 5% of device-sharing edges to verify they represent genuine risk, not fingerprinting artifacts.
- **Policy drift detection**: Compare fraud rates and investigator disposition patterns across training, validation, and test windows. Significant rate changes are noted and contextualized.
- **Graph stability**: Compute network topology statistics (degree, clustering) across periods; large shifts suggest distribution change rather than genuine model improvement.
- **Stratified analysis**: Report performance separately by account age cohort, transaction amount, and (if available) known fraud ring membership, confirming consistency and ruling out overfitting to incidental correlates.

These controls ensure that claimed improvement is robust to label artifacts, policy changes, and graph instability, narrowing the inference to genuine predictive value of transaction networks and device-sharing signals.
difficulty_justification: The case requires domain knowledge to recognize that stratified splitting compromises future-month evaluation validity and that ROC-AUC misrepresents model performance in highly imbalanced contexts, making both flaws subtle yet critical to address.

hypothesis: For ICU sepsis prediction, a transformer model that uses irregularly sampled longitudinal EHR event sequences with time-gap embeddings will predict 12-hour sepsis onset more accurately than a feature-engineered XGBoost baseline trained on aggregated rolling-window vitals and labs.
domain: Hospital intensive care units using electronic health record data for sepsis surveillance
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. XGBoost for 12-Hour Sepsis Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy and Label Definition

The experiment uses 36 months of ICU EHR data from a single hospital system or multi-site network, including all adult admissions (≥18 years) with ≥6 hours of monitored stay. We exclude admissions with sepsis present on arrival or within the first hour to ensure a viable 12-hour prediction horizon.

Raw signals are retained at native resolution: vital signs at 1–5 minute intervals (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, SpO2, temperature); lab results ordered at clinician discretion; medication administrations; and ventilator parameters. All timestamps are preserved; no pre-aggregation occurs.

Sepsis onset is defined as the earlier of: (a) clinician-adjudicated sepsis diagnosis with infection workup and organ dysfunction, or (b) a standardized proxy—suspicion of infection (antibiotic start or blood culture order) within ±4 hours of organ dysfunction (qSOFA ≥2, lactate ≥4 mmol/L, or acute change in urine output or mental status). The prediction task is binary: sepsis within 12 hours from an anchor time (the last observation before the 12-hour window). All labels are assigned retrospectively and clinician-reviewed for consistency.

### 2. Split Strategy and Leakage Prevention

Admissions are partitioned at the admission level using a stratified split that preserves sepsis prevalence, admission type, and site composition across train, validation, and test. We allocate approximately 50% of admissions to training, 25% to validation, and 25% to test, while keeping all events from a given admission within a single split.

This split is intended to ensure that model comparisons are not dominated by imbalance in case mix or seasonal shifts in sepsis prevalence across partitions. Admissions (not individual time points) remain the unit of partitioning, preserving within-admission temporal structure and keeping each sequence intact.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation rules) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test. Specifically:
- Vital signs and labs are scaled using min-max or robust scaling fit on training data.
- Categorical variables (e.g., medication classes) are encoded using a vocabulary fit on training data; unseen categories in validation/test are mapped to an 'unknown' token.
- Missingness is encoded as binary indicators, fit on training set frequencies.
- Time-gap embeddings are computed from inter-event intervals, using quantile bins or sinusoidal encodings fit on the training distribution.

**Transformer input:** Irregularly sampled event sequences. Each event is a 3-tuple (timestamp, type, value) embedded as: (a) learned embedding of event type, (b) normalized value, and (c) learned embedding of time gap since the prior event. Sequences are padded or truncated to 512 events. This representation preserves the irregular, sparse nature of ICU data.

**XGBoost input:** Engineered features from rolling windows (4, 8, 12, 24 hours before anchor time). For each window and vital/lab type: mean, std, min, max, trend (linear slope), missingness ratio, and measurement count. Additional features: age, admission reason, comorbidity count, recent antibiotic exposure. All features are strictly antecedent to the prediction window; no future information leaks.

Both models receive information from the same 12-hour lookback window, ensuring fair comparison.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** 4-layer multi-head self-attention stack (8 heads, 512 hidden dimension, 128-dimensional input embeddings). Positional encoding uses sinusoidal timestamp and index embeddings. Mean pooling aggregates all event tokens; a 2-layer MLP classification head produces the binary prediction. Regularization: dropout (0.2), gradient clipping, label smoothing (α=0.05). Optimizer: Adam (learning rate 1e-4 with warmup), binary cross-entropy loss with class-weight balancing by inverse frequency.

**XGBoost baseline:** Tuned with equivalent effort via random search over 50 hyperparameter combinations (max_depth ∈ [3,10], learning_rate ∈ [0.01,0.3], subsample ∈ [0.6,1.0], colsample_bytree ∈ [0.6,1.0]). Early stopping monitored on validation set (patience=50). Class weights are applied to match the transformer's balancing.

Both models are given equal computational tuning budget and access to the same information window, isolating the hypothesis claim: that irregular sequence modeling improves prediction accuracy over aggregated tabular features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Expected calibration error (ECE) on the test set after Platt scaling on the validation set. ECE is emphasized because the model output will be consumed as a risk probability that informs alerting thresholds, escalation rules, and clinician interpretation; for operational use, probability quality is the most actionable summary of model behavior. A reduction of at least 0.02 in ECE is considered clinically meaningful. The test set result is reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap, n=10,000 resamples).

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision-recall curve and AUPRC (sensitive to minority class imbalance).
- Sensitivity and specificity at the 90% sensitivity threshold (reflects early detection with false positive rate).
- Discrimination: AUROC after validation-set calibration, reported as a secondary measure of ranking quality.
- Time-to-prediction: median hours from anchor to sepsis for true positives in the top 20% by score.
- Subgroup analyses: ECE and AUROC stratified by admission type (surgical vs. medical), baseline severity (APACHE II tertiles), comorbidity burden, and temporal quarters of the test set.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set only. The transformer's checkpoint with lowest validation loss (or best validation ECE after warmup) is retained; XGBoost's best configuration from random search is selected using the same validation criterion after calibration. Early stopping and learning curves are monitored on validation data. The test set is locked and accessed exactly once at the end for final reporting. Calibration is fit on validation data and applied uniformly to both models' test predictions. No re-tuning or feature refinement occurs based on test observations.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Three key confounds are addressed:

**Missingness patterns:** Both models receive explicit missingness indicators. The transformer's attention mechanism can learn to down-weight sparse intervals; XGBoost receives 'proportion missing' features. Both models have equal access to this information, preventing one from exploiting missingness as a proxy.

**Information parity:** Both models are restricted to the same 12-hour lookback window and raw signals (vitals, labs, medications). The transformer does not receive free-text clinical notes or other data inaccessible to the baseline.

**Temporal context:** Both models operate on identical retrospective windows. To isolate the transformer's architectural advantage (versus better irregular sampling handling), a secondary analysis compares the transformer to an LSTM baseline with identical features and tuning effort. Subgroup analyses detect population-level interactions; if the transformer's advantage is concentrated in one subgroup, this signals that confounding interacts with the task and limits generalization.

**Scope:** This design establishes that the transformer improves 12-hour sepsis prediction quality in this hospital system. It does not establish that the transformer generalizes to other institutions, sepsis definitions, or prediction horizons; external validation would be required.

planted_issues.description: Using a stratified split that samples admissions across the full 36-month period for training introduces temporal leakage. The design should use a chronological split to respect real-world deployment where future admissions are predicted using past data, ensuring temporal independence and avoiding entanglement of repeated patient encounters.

Using expected calibration error (ECE) as the primary metric misaligns with the hypothesis about improved prediction accuracy. The hypothesis requires measuring discrimination ability between sepsis and non-sepsis cases, which AUROC captures better than ECE. Favoring calibration could mask poor ranking performance crucial for early detection.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratified split improves case-mix stability and reduces variance in sepsis prevalence across splits
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal ICU data requires chronological splits to avoid leakage of future trends and repeated patient encounters. Stratified splitting on prevalence introduces time-based information leakage that invalidates the prospective evaluation scenario.

must_not_claim.claim: Prioritizing calibration quality (ECE) over pairwise ranking (AUROC) better addresses clinical needs
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis claims improvement in general 'prediction accuracy' for discrimination between cases and controls, which AUROC measures more directly. Early detection requires ranking ability to identify at-risk patients ahead of sepsis onset.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates that the stratified temporal split allows future admissions to influence training, invalidating the prospective evaluation claim. The resolution concludes the split must be chronological with all subsequent admissions in the test period.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Analysis shows the stratified split causes entanglement of time trends across partitions or repeated patient encounters in multiple splits.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Demonstrates stratification was necessary to stabilize class imbalance, though this does not address temporal leakage risks.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic establishes that ECE as the primary metric prioritizes probability calibration over the hypothesis's focus on improved case discrimination. The resolution concludes AUROC (or AUPRC) must remain primary to align with the hypothesis claim.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Data shows poor AUROC performance despite low ECE, or clinical workflows prioritize ranking over absolute probability thresholds for early detection.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. XGBoost for 12-Hour Sepsis Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy and Label Definition

The experiment uses 36 months of ICU EHR data from a single hospital system or multi-site network, including all adult admissions (≥18 years) with ≥6 hours of monitored stay. We exclude admissions with sepsis present on arrival or within the first hour to ensure a viable 12-hour prediction horizon.

Raw signals are retained at native resolution: vital signs at 1–5 minute intervals (heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, SpO2, temperature); lab results ordered at clinician discretion; medication administrations; and ventilator parameters. All timestamps are preserved; no pre-aggregation occurs.

Sepsis onset is defined as the earlier of: (a) clinician-adjudicated sepsis diagnosis with infection workup and organ dysfunction, or (b) a standardized proxy—suspicion of infection (antibiotic start or blood culture order) within ±4 hours of organ dysfunction (qSOFA ≥2, lactate ≥4 mmol/L, or acute change in urine output or mental status). The prediction task is binary: sepsis within 12 hours from an anchor time (the last observation before the 12-hour window). All labels are assigned retrospectively and clinician-reviewed for consistency.

### 2. Split Strategy and Leakage Prevention

Admissions are split chronologically by date, not by individual time points. Training set: admissions from months 1–24. Validation set: admissions from months 25–28. Test set: admissions from months 29–36.

This temporal split is essential because: (1) it respects the deployment scenario where a model trained on historical data is evaluated on prospective admissions; (2) it prevents information leakage from future trends; (3) it avoids entanglement of the same patient's multiple admissions; and (4) it reflects real-world clinical practice drift over time. Admissions (not individual time points) are the unit of randomization, preserving independence.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation rules) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test. Specifically:
- Vital signs and labs are scaled using min-max or robust scaling fit on training data.
- Categorical variables (e.g., medication classes) are encoded using a vocabulary fit on training data; unseen categories in validation/test are mapped to an 'unknown' token.
- Missingness is encoded as binary indicators, fit on training set frequencies.
- Time-gap embeddings are computed from inter-event intervals, using quantile bins or sinusoidal encodings fit on the training distribution.

**Transformer input:** Irregularly sampled event sequences. Each event is a 3-tuple (timestamp, type, value) embedded as: (a) learned embedding of event type, (b) normalized value, and (c) learned embedding of time gap since the prior event. Sequences are padded or truncated to 512 events. This representation preserves the irregular, sparse nature of ICU data.

**XGBoost input:** Engineered features from rolling windows (4, 8, 12, 24 hours before anchor time). For each window and vital/lab type: mean, std, min, max, trend (linear slope), missingness ratio, and measurement count. Additional features: age, admission reason, comorbidity count, recent antibiotic exposure. All features are strictly antecedent to the prediction window; no future information leaks.

Both models receive information from the same 12-hour lookback window, ensuring fair comparison.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** 4-layer multi-head self-attention stack (8 heads, 512 hidden dimension, 128-dimensional input embeddings). Positional encoding uses sinusoidal timestamp and index embeddings. Mean pooling aggregates all event tokens; a 2-layer MLP classification head produces the binary prediction. Regularization: dropout (0.2), gradient clipping, label smoothing (α=0.05). Optimizer: Adam (learning rate 1e-4 with warmup), binary cross-entropy loss with class-weight balancing by inverse frequency.

**XGBoost baseline:** Tuned with equivalent effort via random search over 50 hyperparameter combinations (max_depth ∈ [3,10], learning_rate ∈ [0.01,0.3], subsample ∈ [0.6,1.0], colsample_bytree ∈ [0.6,1.0]). Early stopping monitored on validation set (patience=50). Class weights are applied to match the transformer's balancing.

Both models are given equal computational tuning budget and access to the same information window, isolating the hypothesis claim: that irregular sequence modeling improves prediction accuracy over aggregated tabular features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the receiver-operating-characteristic curve (AUROC) on the test set. AUROC is threshold-independent and robust to class imbalance, allowing the comparison to reflect the models' ranking ability across all clinically relevant operating points. A ≥3 percentage point difference is considered clinically meaningful. The test set result is reported with 95% confidence intervals (via bootstrap, n=10,000 resamples) and a DeLong test p-value.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision-recall curve and AUPRC (sensitive to minority class imbalance).
- Sensitivity and specificity at the 90% sensitivity threshold (reflects early detection with false positive rate).
- Calibration: Expected calibration error (ECE) and Brier score after Platt scaling on the validation set.
- Time-to-prediction: median hours from anchor to sepsis for true positives in the top 20% by score.
- Subgroup analyses: AUROC stratified by admission type (surgical vs. medical), baseline severity (APACHE II tertiles), comorbidity burden, and temporal quarters of the test set.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set only. The transformer's checkpoint with lowest validation loss (or best validation AUROC) is retained; XGBoost's best configuration from random search is selected. Early stopping and learning curves are monitored on validation data. The test set is locked and accessed exactly once at the end for final reporting. Calibration is fit on validation data and applied uniformly to both models' test predictions. No re-tuning or feature refinement occurs based on test observations.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

Three key confounds are addressed:

**Missingness patterns:** Both models receive explicit missingness indicators. The transformer's attention mechanism can learn to down-weight sparse intervals; XGBoost receives 'proportion missing' features. Both models have equal access to this information, preventing one from exploiting missingness as a proxy.

**Information parity:** Both models are restricted to the same 12-hour lookback window and raw signals (vitals, labs, medications). The transformer does not receive free-text clinical notes or other data inaccessible to the baseline.

**Temporal context:** Both models operate on identical retrospective windows. To isolate the transformer's architectural advantage (versus better irregular sampling handling), a secondary analysis compares the transformer to an LSTM baseline with identical features and tuning effort. Subgroup analyses detect population-level interactions; if the transformer's advantage is concentrated in one subgroup, this signals that confounding interacts with the task and limits generalization.

**Scope:** This design establishes that the transformer improves 12-hour sepsis prediction accuracy in this hospital system. It does not establish that the transformer generalizes to other institutions, sepsis definitions, or prediction horizons; external validation would be required.
difficulty_justification: This case is non-trivial because the flaws involve decisions that appear reasonable without domain expertise: stabilizing case-mix through stratification and prioritizing calibration for clinical usability. However, understanding ICU data's temporal dependencies and the hypothesis's focus on discrimination reveals the critical flaws.

hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network trained on user-item interaction sequences will rank next-item recommendations better than a static matrix-factorization baseline for a fast-moving e-commerce marketplace, as measured by held-out session-level click-through and conversion ranking quality.
domain: E-commerce marketplace recommendation with sessionized clickstream and catalog data
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. Static Factorization for E-commerce Ranking

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

We would collect 12 months of production clickstream data from the e-commerce platform, including all recommendation impressions, clicks, cart additions, purchases, session metadata (user ID, session ID, timestamp, referral source), and item metadata (category, price, brand, availability, ingestion date). A session is defined as all interactions by a single user within a 30-minute window or until they leave the site.

Labels are derived from implicit feedback: a recommended item is labeled *positive* if the user clicked it or added it to cart/purchased within 30 minutes of the impression (same session). Items shown but not engaged within 24 hours are labeled *negative*. This definition aligns with the business goal: we care about whether the recommendation prompted an immediate user action. Items not shown to any user are excluded; we make no assumptions about counterfactual preferences.

### 2. Split Strategy

We use a **session-level stratified split** so that train, validation, and test preserve the same engagement rate and traffic composition:
- **Training:** approximately 8 months' worth of sessions sampled from the 12-month log
- **Validation:** approximately 2 months' worth of sessions for hyperparameter tuning and model selection
- **Test:** approximately 2 months' worth of sessions held out until final evaluation

Stratification is applied over key sources of variation that materially affect recommendation difficulty, including positive-label prevalence, referral source, and exposure to cold-start items. This keeps all folds representative of the same operating mixture and avoids allocating entire seasonal regimes or traffic modes to a single split. Since the hypothesis is about session-level ranking quality rather than forecasting performance for one particular calendar interval, preserving comparable candidate-pool composition across folds provides a more stable basis for model comparison.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformations (scalers, categorical encoders, interaction vocabularies) are fit only on training data and then applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Vocabulary for user IDs, item IDs, and categories is learned from the training set; unseen users or items in validation/test are mapped to reserved OOV tokens.

Constructed features are:
- **User-level:** session count in past 7 and 30 days, unique categories browsed, avg. items per session
- **Item-level:** popularity (click count, impression count in past 7 and 30 days), price decile, category, brand, availability flag, days since addition to catalog
- **Session-level:** current session length, referral source, time of day, day of week
- **Interaction-level:** user-item co-occurrence in past 30 days, user affinity for item's category, time since last user-item interaction
- **Position bias:** explicit impression rank (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) in recommendation slot

All time windows are computed relative to the impression timestamp, ensuring no future data is used. Position bias features are included explicitly so both models can learn and correct for position effects on an equal footing.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Proposed Model:** A Temporal Graph Neural Network (TGNN) that models user-item interactions as a dynamic bipartite graph with timestamp-encoded edges. The model processes the sequence of interactions within each session, using temporal message-passing layers to aggregate user and item embeddings, and produces a ranking score for each candidate item. It is trained end-to-end on the training set to predict binary engagement (click/purchase vs. no engagement) using cross-entropy loss, with early stopping on validation-set AUC.

**Baseline:** Static matrix factorization (SVD-based collaborative filtering) trained on the same training interaction matrix (clicks and purchases). The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: both user and item embeddings are learned via SVD on the training set; for new users or items in validation/test, embeddings are initialized via content features (user history and item metadata). Both models include learned position bias correction (same position-rank features as the TGNN). Hyperparameter search (embedding dimension, regularization, learning rate) is performed with equal grid-search budget for both, tuned on validation-set AUROC.

This ensures the baseline is not strawmanned: it uses the same features, the same bias correction, and receives the same tuning investment.

### 5. Primary Metric

**Per-impression AUROC (Area Under the ROC Curve):** For each evaluation fold, we score every impressed candidate item and compute AUROC over the binary engagement label, then aggregate across all impressions.

This metric is appropriate because recommendation traffic is heterogeneous: referral source, session depth, and candidate-pool size all change the base rate of engagement. Evaluating discrimination at the impression level makes performance comparable across these slices without overweighting sessions with unusually small or large candidate sets. It also supports clean subgroup analysis using the same scoring definition across user cohorts, traffic sources, and catalog segments.

### 6. Secondary Metrics and Controls

We also compute:
- **MRR@5** (Mean Reciprocal Rank): whether *any* engagement occurs in the top 5
- **Position-stratified AUROC:** performance separately by impression rank (1–5) to verify improvement is not driven by learning position bias alone
- **Recency-stratified AUROC:** performance for new users, returning users (7 days), and power users to detect fairness issues
- **Conversion rate (top-5):** fraction of sessions where a top-5 item resulted in purchase, not just a click
- **Popularity-stratified AUROC:** performance on high-popularity items vs. low-popularity items to verify gains are not due to temporal trends
- **Catalog-stratified AUROC:** performance on established items (>30 days old) vs. new items to verify robustness to catalog churn

These stratified metrics allow us to check whether improvements are confounded by popularity trends, position bias, user cohorts, or catalog churn. Improvements that hold across all strata are more credible.

### 7. Model Selection and Evaluation Protocol

Both models are tuned on the validation set using per-impression AUROC as the selection metric. Early stopping for the TGNN also uses validation-set AUC. Once hyperparameters are fixed, both models are evaluated exactly once on the test set under identical conditions. No post-hoc adjustments, retuning, or cherry-picking are permitted after test evaluation.

### 8. Scope and Claims

This experiment establishes that the TGNN produces better impression-level discrimination and stronger recommendation quality than static matrix factorization on held-out sessions, controlling for popularity trends, position bias, and user cohorts. It does **not** establish causality (would require A/B testing in production) or isolate which aspects of the TGNN (sequence length, temporal encoding, graph structure) drive the improvement (would require ablation studies). The comparison is fair and the test set is unbiased, supporting a confident claim of ranking quality improvement in this offline setting.

planted_issues.description: The use of session-level stratified sampling across the full 12-month log introduces temporal leakage by mixing future interaction patterns into training. This invalidates the evaluation because time-sensitive features like item popularity and user recency will be trained on future data, inflating offline performance estimates.

Using per-impression AUROC as the primary metric misaligns with the business goal of ranking relevant items in top session-specific positions. AUROC measures global pairwise discrimination and rewards models that rank high-popularity items globally rather than correctly reordering session-specific candidates.

must_not_claim.claim: Including position bias correction as explicit features introduces unfairness between the TGNN and baseline
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Both models receive identical position bias correction mechanisms (impression rank features), ensuring equal footing in addressing position effects. Claiming unfairness would ignore the fair comparison framework.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies the temporal leakage flaw in the session-level stratified split and argues that chronological splitting is required to evaluate generalization across time. Defense provides no valid rebuttal, as temporal leakage directly invalidates performance claims.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Temporal leakage is demonstrated by tracking feature dependencies on future data (e.g., popularity features updated beyond training window)
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: null

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies the metric mismatch flaw, arguing that session-level NDCG@5 is required to evaluate top-5 ranking relevance within sessions rather than global AUROC. Defense provides no valid rebuttal, as AUROC cannot measure session-specific ranking quality.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Session-level ranking failures are demonstrated with examples where high AUROC scores coexist with poor top-5 rankings
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: null

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. Static Factorization for E-commerce Ranking

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

We would collect 12 months of production clickstream data from the e-commerce platform, including all recommendation impressions, clicks, cart additions, purchases, session metadata (user ID, session ID, timestamp, referral source), and item metadata (category, price, brand, availability, ingestion date). A session is defined as all interactions by a single user within a 30-minute window or until they leave the site.

Labels are derived from implicit feedback: a recommended item is labeled *positive* if the user clicked it or added it to cart/purchased within 30 minutes of the impression (same session). Items shown but not engaged within 24 hours are labeled *negative*. This definition aligns with the business goal: we care about whether the recommendation prompted an immediate user action. Items not shown to any user are excluded; we make no assumptions about counterfactual preferences.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

We use a **chronological split** to avoid temporal leakage:
- **Training:** Months 1–8 (full interaction history and item metadata as of those dates)
- **Validation:** Months 9–10 (for hyperparameter tuning and model selection)
- **Test:** Months 11–12 (held-out, touched only once at the end)

This split is essential because e-commerce is non-stationary: item popularity evolves, the catalog changes (new items added, old items archived), user behavior drifts, and the production ranking system itself may be updated. A random split would leak future information into training (e.g., items that become popular only in month 11 would be treated as predictive in month 5), inflating offline metrics and harming real-world deployment. Chronological split ensures the model never trains on future catalog state, future popularity trends, or future user behavior.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformations (scalers, categorical encoders, interaction vocabularies) are fit only on training data (months 1–8) and then applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Vocabulary for user IDs, item IDs, and categories is learned from the training set; unseen users or items in validation/test are mapped to reserved OOV tokens.

Constructed features are:
- **User-level:** session count in past 7 and 30 days, unique categories browsed, avg. items per session
- **Item-level:** popularity (click count, impression count in past 7 and 30 days), price decile, category, brand, availability flag, days since addition to catalog
- **Session-level:** current session length, referral source, time of day, day of week
- **Interaction-level:** user-item co-occurrence in past 30 days, user affinity for item's category, time since last user-item interaction
- **Position bias:** explicit impression rank (1, 2, 3, 4, 5) in recommendation slot

All time windows are computed relative to the impression timestamp, ensuring no future data is used. Position bias features are included explicitly so both models can learn and correct for position effects on an equal footing.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Proposed Model:** A Temporal Graph Neural Network (TGNN) that models user-item interactions as a dynamic bipartite graph with timestamp-encoded edges. The model processes the sequence of interactions within each session, using temporal message-passing layers to aggregate user and item embeddings, and produces a ranking score for each candidate item. It is trained end-to-end on the training set to predict binary engagement (click/purchase vs. no engagement) using cross-entropy loss, with early stopping on validation-set AUC.

**Baseline:** Static matrix factorization (SVD-based collaborative filtering) trained on the same training interaction matrix (clicks and purchases). The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort: both user and item embeddings are learned via SVD on the training set; for new users or items in validation/test, embeddings are initialized via content features (user history and item metadata). Both models include learned position bias correction (same position-rank features as the TGNN). Hyperparameter search (embedding dimension, regularization, learning rate) is performed with equal grid-search budget for both, tuned on validation-set NDCG@5.

This ensures the baseline is not strawmanned: it uses the same features, the same bias correction, and receives the same tuning investment.

### 5. Primary Metric

**Session-level NDCG@5 (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at rank 5):** For each session, we rank the candidate items by model score and compute NDCG as the normalized sum of relevance (1 if clicked/purchased, 0 otherwise) weighted by log-discount of position. NDCG@5 is averaged across all test-set sessions.

This metric directly aligns with the business objective: it measures whether the highest-ranked items are the ones users actually engage with, and it weights top positions more heavily (reflecting that users examine recommendations from top to bottom). For fast-moving e-commerce, users typically review only the first few recommendations, making top-heavy metrics appropriate.

### 6. Secondary Metrics and Controls

We also compute:
- **MRR@5** (Mean Reciprocal Rank): whether *any* engagement occurs in the top 5
- **Position-stratified NDCG:** performance separately by impression rank (1–5) to verify improvement is not driven by learning position bias alone
- **Recency-stratified NDCG:** performance for new users, returning users (7 days), and power users to detect fairness issues
- **Conversion rate (top-5):** fraction of sessions where a top-5 item resulted in purchase, not just a click
- **Popularity-stratified NDCG:** performance on high-popularity items vs. low-popularity items to verify gains are not due to temporal trends
- **Catalog-stratified NDCG:** performance on established items (>30 days old) vs. new items to verify robustness to catalog churn

These stratified metrics allow us to check whether improvements are confounded by popularity trends, position bias, user cohorts, or catalog churn. Improvements that hold across all strata are more credible.

### 7. Model Selection and Evaluation Protocol

Both models are tuned on the validation set (months 9–10) using session-level NDCG@5 as the selection metric. Early stopping for the TGNN uses validation-set AUC. Once hyperparameters are fixed, both models are evaluated exactly once on the test set (months 11–12) under identical conditions. No post-hoc adjustments, retuning, or cherry-picking are permitted after test evaluation.

### 8. Scope and Claims

This experiment establishes that the TGNN produces better session-level rankings than static matrix factorization on held-out future sessions, controlling for popularity trends, position bias, and user cohorts. It does **not** establish causality (would require A/B testing in production) or isolate which aspects of the TGNN (sequence length, temporal encoding, graph structure) drive the improvement (would require ablation studies). The comparison is fair and the test set is unbiased, supporting a confident claim of ranking quality improvement in this offline setting.
difficulty_justification: Both issues are subtle methodological flaws in non-stationary data evaluation and metric alignment. Detecting them requires understanding time-ordered model evaluation and business-aligned ranking metrics, but the corrupted design provides superficial justification for its choices.

hypothesis: For a credit card issuer’s fraud detection system, a model that combines transaction sequences with customer-device graph features will detect fraudulent transactions with higher recall at the same false-positive rate than a baseline gradient-boosted model using only tabular transaction aggregates.
domain: Retail banking fraud detection using live card-not-present and card-present transaction logs from a large issuer
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence + Graph Features for Fraud Detection

The hypothesis claims that a model combining transaction sequences with customer-device graph features will detect fraud with higher recall at fixed false-positive rate than a gradient-boosted baseline using only tabular aggregates. To test this rigorously, we propose the following design.

### 1. Data and Label Strategy

We use 36 months of production transaction logs (Jan 2021–Dec 2023) from the issuer's systems. Each transaction includes amount, merchant category, timestamp, device fingerprint, IP geolocation, and cardholder ID. Fraud labels come from chargeback reports and issuer investigations, with a known labeling delay of 30–90 days. To respect label maturity, we exclude all transactions occurring less than 180 days before the data extraction cutoff, ensuring that training and evaluation use only transactions with complete label information and avoiding the bias of censored outcomes.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split

We employ a temporal split stratified by transaction type (card-present vs. card-not-present):
- **Training:** Months 1–18 (Jan 2021–Jun 2022)
- **Validation:** Months 19–24 (Jul–Dec 2022)
- **Test:** Months 25–36 (Jan–Dec 2023)

Temporal splitting is essential: it prevents the model from learning patterns from future transactions and reflects the real-world deployment scenario in which a model trained on historical data is applied to new, unseen time periods. Stratification by transaction type ensures both card-present and card-not-present fraud mechanisms are represented proportionally in all three sets, since fraud patterns differ significantly between channels.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Leakage Control

The baseline feature set includes transaction amount, merchant category, time-of-day, account age, 7-day and 30-day velocity metrics, historical fraud rate, device count, and IP-country mismatch. All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets.

The enhanced feature set adds two groups: (i) **Sequence features:** a recency-weighted embedding of the last 10 transactions per account, capturing merchant-category transitions, inter-transaction time deltas, and amount patterns; (ii) **Graph features:** device clustering coefficient, shortest path distance to known fraud devices, device-merchant affinity (computed from training set fraud labels only), and account-device-merchant motif patterns in the device-merchant subgraph.

Critically, all graph statistics are time-bounded: for any transaction at time T, we use only edges and fraud labels from transactions at or before time T. This is strictly enforced and audited to prevent future information leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline Comparison

The primary model combines a transformer sequence encoder (3 layers, 64 hidden units) on the last 10 transactions, graph features (12-dim), and baseline tabular features (15-dim). These three representations are concatenated and passed through three fully connected layers (256, 128, 64 units with dropout) to a sigmoid output. The model is trained with class-weighted cross-entropy loss to reflect the extreme class imbalance.

The baseline is an XGBoost model trained on baseline features only. Crucially, both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search over learning rate, tree depth/regularization, batch size on the validation set), the same training data, the same class-weighting scheme, and evaluation under identical test conditions. This ensures that any performance difference is attributable to feature richness and model capacity, not unequal tuning or data access.

### 5. Primary Metric and Operational Justification

We use **AUC-ROC** as the primary metric, computed on the test set. The main reason is that the fraud queue will ultimately support multiple downstream operating points across issuers, card products, and manual-review capacities, so the comparison should focus first on whether the model produces a stronger global ranking of risky transactions rather than on a single threshold tied to one review budget. This also makes the ablation and channel-level comparisons easier to interpret because discrimination can be compared on a common scale even when the effective alert volume differs across segments.

Secondary metrics include precision at 1% FPR, recall at adjacent FPR thresholds (0.5%, 2%), recall at 1% FPR as an operating-point diagnostic, and calibration (Brier score, calibration curve), which are important for downstream alert prioritization and robustness checks.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity

Hyperparameters are selected by grid search using validation AUC-ROC. The final model is then evaluated on the held-out test set to produce the primary and secondary metrics. The test set is touched only once, after all development is complete, to produce final metrics. No iterative refinement or secondary tuning using test performance is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

We address the confound that improved graph features may reflect better entity resolution or recent fraud intelligence rather than sequence value:

1. **Ablation:** Train a third model on baseline + graph features only (no sequence). If this performs nearly as well as the primary model, sequence features are not the driver.
2. **Temporal stratification:** Report AUC-ROC separately for early, middle, and late test periods, along with recall at 1% FPR as a supporting operating-point check. Sharp degradation suggests data drift or leakage.
3. **Graph-leakage audit:** Sample 500 test transactions and verify that no graph edges or labels post-date the transaction timestamp.
4. **Channel breakdown:** Report metrics separately for card-present and card-not-present transactions; channel-specific improvements may indicate confounds.

A positive result is claimed only if the primary model outperforms the baseline on the test set on AUC-ROC, the ablation does not explain the gap, temporal stratification is stable, and the leakage audit passes.

planted_issues.description: The design replaces the hypothesis-aligned operationally meaningful metric (recall at fixed 1% false-positive rate) with AUC-ROC. This fundamentally misaligns evaluation with the business objective of catching more fraud at a specific operational false alert budget. A model can achieve higher AUC-ROC while performing worse at the critical 1% FPR threshold, making this a high-severity methodological flaw.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratification by transaction type introduces overfitting or unnecessary complexity
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Card-present and card-not-present fraud mechanisms differ significantly in patterns and prevalence. Stratification ensures both channels are proportionally represented in all data splits, which is required to generalize detection performance across payment channels relevant to the hypothesis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic correctly identifies metric mismatch as a critical flaw and establishes that AUC-ROC does not guarantee improved performance at the stated operational threshold (1% FPR). Verdict requires recognizing the irreconcilable misalignment between the chosen metric and the hypothesis's operational constraints.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains that AUC-ROC assesses overall ranking quality but fails to ensure performance gains at the critical operating point where fraud detection must balance between alert volume and detection efficacy.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: No defensible mitigation exists because the metric mismatch directly contradicts the hypothesis's stated success condition regarding fixed false-positive rate constraints.

To respect label maturity, we exclude all transactions occurring less than 180 days before the data extraction cutoff, ensuring that training and evaluation use only transactions with complete label information and avoiding the bias of censored outcomes.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split

We employ a temporal split stratified by transaction type (card-present vs. card-not-present):
- **Training:** Months 1–18 (Jan 2021–Jun 2022)
- **Validation:** Months 19–24 (Jul–Dec 2022)
- **Test:** Months 25–36 (Jan–Dec 2023)

Stratification by transaction type ensures both card-present and card-not-present fraud mechanisms are represented proportionally in all three sets, since fraud patterns differ significantly between channels.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Leakage Control

The baseline feature set includes transaction amount, merchant category, time-of-day, account age, 7-day and 30-day velocity metrics, historical fraud rate, device count, and IP-country mismatch. All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets.

The enhanced feature set adds two groups: (i) **Sequence features:** a recency-weighted embedding of the last 10 transactions per account, capturing merchant-category transitions, inter-transaction time deltas, and amount patterns; (ii) **Graph features:** device clustering coefficient, shortest path distance to known fraud devices, device-merchant affinity (computed from training set fraud labels only), and account-device-merchant motif patterns in the device-merchant subgraph.

Critically, all graph statistics are time-bounded: for any transaction at time T, we use only edges and fraud labels from transactions at or before time T. This is strictly enforced and audited to prevent future information leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline Comparison

The primary model combines a transformer sequence encoder (3 layers, 64 hidden units) on the last 10 transactions, graph features (12-dim), and baseline tabular features (15-dim). These three representations are concatenated and passed through three fully connected layers (256, 128, 64 units with dropout) to a sigmoid output. The baseline is an XGBoost model trained on baseline features only. Crucially, both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search over learning rate, tree depth/regularization, batch size on the validation set), the same training data, the same class-weighting scheme, and evaluation under identical test conditions. This ensures that any performance difference is attributable to feature richness and model capacity, not unequal tuning or data access.

### 5. Primary Metric and Operational Justification

We use **recall at a fixed false-positive rate (FPR) of 1%** as the primary metric, computed on the test set. This metric directly aligns with the business objective: 1% FPR operationalizes the acceptable customer friction budget (roughly 1 false alert per 100 legitimate transactions), and recall at this operating point measures fraud-catching efficacy. This avoids the opacity of AUC and forces an explicit operational tradeoff rather than assuming all misclassification errors have equal cost.

Secondary metrics include precision at 1% FPR, recall at adjacent FPR thresholds (0.5%, 2%), and calibration (Brier score, calibration curve), which are important for downstream alert prioritization and robustness checks.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Integrity

Hyperparameters are selected by grid search using recall at 1% FPR on the validation set. The FPR threshold is set on validation and applied to the test set without retraining. The test set is touched only once, after all development is complete, to produce final metrics. No iterative refinement or secondary tuning using test performance is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

We address the confound that improved graph features may reflect better entity resolution or recent fraud intelligence rather than sequence value:

1. **Ablation:** Train a third model on baseline + graph features only (no sequence). If this performs nearly as well as the primary model, sequence features are not the driver.
2. **Temporal stratification:** Report recall at 1% FPR separately for early, middle, and late test periods. Sharp degradation suggests data drift or leakage.
3. **Graph-leakage audit:** Sample 500 test transactions and verify that no graph edges or labels post-date the transaction timestamp.
4. **Channel breakdown:** Report metrics separately for card-present and card-not-present transactions; channel-specific improvements may indicate confounds.

A positive result is claimed only if the primary model outperforms the baseline on the test set, the ablation does not explain the gap, temporal stratification is stable, and the leakage audit passes.
difficulty_justification: The corruption is subtle because it maintains metric validity in general but mismatches the hypothesis's operational constraints. It requires domain understanding to realize that AUC-ROC does not guarantee performance improvement at a specific false-positive rate budget critical to the business objective.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, a sequence model that ingests ordered event logs and alert metadata will outperform a tabular gradient-boosted baseline on predicting whether an alert is confirmed malicious within 24 hours, especially when evaluated on future time periods rather than random splits.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity operations using SIEM/EDR alert streams and incident response tickets
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Gradient Boosting for Cybersecurity Alert Triage

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 24–36 months of historical alerts from the enterprise SIEM/EDR platform, sourced from a single SOC environment. Each alert record includes a timestamp, ordered event log (the sequence of raw security events that triggered the rule), SIEM metadata (rule ID, severity, host/user identifiers), and analyst disposition from incident response tickets.

The ground truth label is defined as: **confirmed malicious = 1** if an analyst investigated the alert and the associated incident ticket indicates confirmed malicious activity (intrusion, suspicious payload, data exfiltration) within 72 hours of alert generation. Alerts marked as benign/false positive by an analyst are labeled 0. Critically, alerts with no analyst review and no mention in incident postmortems are **excluded** from both training and test sets, rather than assumed negative. This prevents labeling bias from high-volume, low-priority alerts that were never reviewed. The test set uses only alerts with complete disposition information available at evaluation time, ensuring labels are ground truth and not subject to future updates.

### 2. Train-Validation-Test Split

The dataset is partitioned by stratifying alerts across the full observation horizon on both the malicious label and coarse alert taxonomy, then assigning 50%/25%/25% to train, validation, and test:
- **Training set:** 50% of alerts. Used to fit the sequence model, baseline model, and downstream predictors.
- **Validation set:** 25% of alerts. Used for hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and any threshold-setting analysis attached to secondary reporting.
- **Test set:** 25% of alerts. Held out until final evaluation.

This partitioning is intended to preserve the operational alert mix in each split, particularly because some rule families and severity strata occur in short bursts and would otherwise be unevenly represented. By balancing both label prevalence and rule-family composition, the comparison between the sequence model and the tabular baseline is less sensitive to one split containing a disproportionate share of rare but high-impact alerts.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models are given the same tabular features to ensure a fair comparison of sequential representation vs. tabular representation. The feature set includes:
- SIEM rule ID (one-hot encoded)
- Alert severity (ordinal, normalized)
- Host and user risk scores (if available, standardized to [0, 1])
- Time-of-day and day-of-week (sine/cosine encoded)
- Count of alerts from the same host in the preceding 24 hours
- Count of alerts from the same user in the preceding 24 hours

Preprocessing parameters are established once at dataset construction time and then frozen so that every candidate model operates on an identical feature space. Continuous variables use a single set of normalization and imputation parameters, categorical vocabularies are finalized before model training, and the same fitted transformations are applied throughout training, validation, and test evaluation.

The sequence model additionally receives the ordered event log preceding the alert: up to 50 events, each represented as (event_type, timestamp_delta_from_alert, source_ip, destination_ip, user_id). Events are zero-padded or truncated to 50; padding/truncation limits are selected from corpus-level event-length quantiles so memory and batching behavior stay consistent across experiments. The baseline (gradient boosted tree) receives aggregate sequence statistics (e.g., event count in 24h window, most common event type) instead of raw sequences, ensuring both models have access to the same conceptual information but in different representations.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Sequence model:** A bidirectional LSTM or Transformer encoder with 128 units, 1 attention head, and dropout 0.3. The sequence is encoded to a fixed vector, concatenated with tabular features, and passed through a dense layer with sigmoid output. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss, Adam optimizer, and early stopping on validation loss (patience=10 epochs, max 100 epochs).

**Baseline:** LightGBM trained on tabular features plus aggregate sequence statistics. The baseline uses a compact, fixed configuration selected in advance, with shallow trees, moderate learning rate, and early stopping on validation loss. The goal is to keep the reference model stable and interpretable rather than heavily optimize it to a particular validation partition, so the comparison centers on whether explicit sequential modeling adds value over a strong lightweight tabular learner.

If the sequence model outperforms the baseline, the gain is interpreted as evidence that the ordered event context contains predictive structure not captured by tabular summaries alone.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** AUROC on the test set. In this setting, SOC load and escalation policy can vary by week and by rule family, so threshold-specific metrics may shift with operational policy even when the underlying score quality remains stable. AUROC therefore serves as the main comparison metric because it measures whether malicious alerts are systematically ranked above benign alerts across those varying operating conditions.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Expected Calibration Error (reliability of predicted probabilities)
- Precision-Recall curve (performance across multiple operating points)
- Precision at 90% recall
- Recall at fixed false positive rate (e.g., FPR=10%)

### 6. Confound Controls

To isolate the effect of sequential representation: (1) Identical tabular features and preprocessing for both models; sequence model receives additional sequence input. (2) Both models are trained and evaluated on partitions with matched label prevalence and alert taxonomy composition. (3) If multi-site data are available, train and evaluate per-site separately to check generalization. (4) Stratification by severity and rule type to detect if gains concentrate in a subset. (5) Bias check: alert label distribution (% confirmed malicious) is compared across train, validation, and test partitions to document any residual differences.

planted_issues.description: Temporal leakage in data partitioning: Stratifying alerts across the entire observation horizon allows models to learn patterns from future data, invalidating the evaluation's ability to measure generalization to future alerts. This fundamentally misaligns with the hypothesis's focus on sequential modeling advantages over random splits, which cannot simulate real-world deployment.

Preprocessing leakage from using full-dataset statistics: Calculating normalization, imputation, and sequence padding parameters on the entire dataset encodes future information into training. This creates an artificially stable feature distribution, inflating test performance while masking concept drift challenges critical to real-world triage operations.

Unfair baseline comparison: Using a fixed, untuned configuration for the LightGBM baseline weakens its predictive capability, making any sequence model superiority artificially easy to demonstrate. This obscures whether gains come from sequential representation or suboptimal baseline design.

Inauthentic primary metric: Prioritizing AUROC over precision-at-90%-recall abandons the hypothesis's operational requirement of minimizing false positives under fixed analyst capacity. AUROC rewards global ranking ability while ignoring performance at the critical threshold defining analyst workflow.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratified train-validation-test split improves evaluation by maintaining rule-family proportionality.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In time-dependent cybersecurity data, temporal splits are fundamental to simulate deployment conditions. Stratification across periods allows models to memorize future patterns, violating the hypothesis's requirement to evaluate generalization to unseen future alerts.

must_not_claim.claim: Global preprocessing parameters stabilize feature distributions and reduce training complexity.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In temporal problems, preprocessing fit on future data leaks distributional information, making test performance appear optimistically clean. Correct evaluation requires training-set-only statistics to reflect actual deployment constraints.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic demonstrates that stratified splits and full-dataset preprocessing jointly enable temporal leakage, invalidating the evaluation's temporal validity. Final verdict requires rejection of both design choices and advocacy for strict temporal validation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Review reveals that models implicitly rely on future patterns encoded in stratified splits and preprocessing parameters, which cannot exist in real-time deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Not applicable — this flaw has no valid remediation without design changes.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic proves that the untuned baseline creates an unfair comparison baseline and demands equivalence in hyperparameter optimization efforts for the sequence model and baseline.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Empirical evidence shows baseline gains from tuning invalidate the original conclusion, proving gains stemmed from model selection bias.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Not applicable — this flaw has no valid remediation without design changes.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic argues that AUROC fails to reflect the operational requirement of precision-at-90%-recall and insists on metric alignment with analyst workflow constraints.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Empirical analysis shows that models with similar AUROC perform drastically differently under the precision-at-90%-recall requirement, necessitating metric change.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Not applicable — this flaw has no valid remediation without design changes.
