The experiment will establish whether a temporal GNN outperforms text-only models at predicting on-site interviews for future requisitions. It will not establish causality or determine whether the improvement reflects true candidate quality or recruiter/manager behavior patterns; stratified and ablation analyses clarify this distinction.
difficulty_justification: The evaluation requires domain-specific understanding of recruiter workflow constraints to recognize that ROC-AUC, despite its generality, is a flawed choice for prioritizing candidates under capacity limitations. The stratified metrics and confound controls provide misleading cover for the primary metric mismatch, making this a non-trivial assessment.

hypothesis: For a hospital intranet/physician knowledge portal, a neural dual-encoder retriever fine-tuned on click-through and dwell-time logs will retrieve clinically relevant documents more effectively than a BM25 baseline, as measured by nDCG@10 on future physician queries.
domain: Clinical information retrieval in a hospital intranet/physician knowledge portal
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Neural Dual-Encoder vs. BM25 for Clinical Retrieval

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 24 months of production query logs from the hospital knowledge portal. Each interaction record contains a timestamped physician query, the set of documents presented to the physician, which document(s) were clicked, dwell time on clicked documents, and metadata about the document (type, creation/update date). All data are pseudonymized and conform to institutional data governance policies.

Labels for training are inferred from implicit feedback: a click on a document is treated as a positive signal; absence of a click is a negative. Dwell time (>30 seconds) serves as a secondary confirmation of relevance, though the primary training signal is the click itself. However, because click logs are biased by position and document prominence, we use expert annotation for all evaluation. A panel of five clinicians (emergency medicine, internal medicine, surgery) will independently annotate 500 randomly sampled query-document pairs sampled from the test period. Pairs are annotated on a three-point scale (not relevant, somewhat relevant, highly relevant) and blinded to retriever identity. Inter-rater agreement (Fleiss' kappa) is computed; disagreements are resolved by majority vote. These expert labels are used only for evaluation, never for model training, ensuring an unbiased assessment of true retrieval quality.

### 2. Data Splits and Temporal Strategy

Data are partitioned at the query level across the full 24-month interaction log: 70% of interactions for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for held-out test. Partitioning is stratified jointly by query length (short: <5 tokens, medium: 5–15 tokens, long: >15 tokens) and calendar quarter so that each split contains comparable mixtures of query complexity and seasonal demand. This design keeps differences between train, validation, and test from being dominated by any single contiguous period and makes the comparison focus on retrieval quality under the full range of observed portal usage patterns.

Within each split, the same query-length bands are preserved to ensure balanced representation. This prevents either model from exploiting query-length artifacts as a shortcut. Query length effects are monitored as a secondary metric to ensure gains are not driven by better handling of a single query type.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

No hand-engineered features are constructed. For the neural dual-encoder, raw query and document text (title + body concatenated) are tokenized and embedded via learned representations. Document metadata (type, recency, update frequency, prominence in the portal) are available but deliberately excluded from both the neural model and BM25 baseline, to isolate semantic retrieval quality and prevent gains from being driven by metadata shortcuts (e.g., preferring recently updated documents).

All preprocessing steps (tokenization, lowercasing, stop-word removal, punctuation normalization) are fit on training data only. The fitted tokenizer and vocabulary are then applied identically to validation and test data; no statistics are recomputed on held-out sets. For BM25, standard TF-IDF weighting with no query expansion is used. Hyperparameters are set to widely accepted defaults (k1=1.5, b=0.75) to ensure fair comparison and to avoid the appearance that the baseline was deliberately weakened through poor tuning.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a dual-encoder transformer (candidate: DistilBERT or a clinically adapted BERT checkpoint). The encoder processes queries and documents separately into fixed-size embedding vectors; retrieval is performed via nearest-neighbor search in embedding space (cosine similarity or a learned similarity function). Training uses a contrastive loss (InfoNCE or margin-based ranking loss) on clicked query-document pairs, with in-batch negatives and hard negatives mined from the training set during training.

The baseline is BM25, a standard lexical retrieval method, given identical preprocessing and no tuning effort beyond standard parameter settings. BM25 is an appropriate baseline because it is the current production system (or the simplest strong alternative) and represents the status quo; the hypothesis claims the neural model will exceed it, not merely that neural retrieval is feasible.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** query-level AUROC, computed on expert-labeled ground truth from the test set. For each query, model scores over the annotated query-document pairs are compared against expert relevance labels, and AUROC is averaged across queries. This metric is chosen because it captures how consistently each retriever separates relevant from non-relevant documents across the candidate set, while remaining stable for subgroup analyses where the number of annotated relevant documents per query may vary.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall@10: fraction of relevant documents retrieved in the top 10.
- MRR@10: position of the first relevant result, capturing whether top results are useful.
- nDCG@10 stratified by query length: reveals whether gains are uniform or concentrated in certain query types (e.g., the neural model might excel on long queries but underperform on short ones).
- In a live deployment phase, click-through rate and dwell time on retrieved documents are monitored to validate that offline improvements translate to changed physician behavior.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set. A subset of 200 validation queries are expert-annotated (by the same panel, blind to condition). The primary metric for model selection is query-level AUROC on these expert-annotated validation queries. Early stopping and hyperparameter sweeps (embedding dimension, learning rate, batch size, contrastive loss temperature) are guided by validation AUROC. The model checkpoint with the highest validation AUROC is selected and evaluated exactly once on the locked test set. The test set is not inspected during training or tuning; no intermediate results inform model selection.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope

**Position bias:** Clicks are biased toward the top-ranked document regardless of true relevance. This is controlled by using expert labels for evaluation (not clicks). During training, hard-negative mining—deliberately selecting non-clicked high-relevance documents as negatives—helps the model learn to distinguish truly relevant from merely prominent documents.

**Document popularity and freshness:** Both models are given identical features (raw text only), preventing the neural model from exploiting document recency or popularity. If the neural model outperforms BM25, the gain cannot be attributed to leveraging metadata shortcuts.

**Query-length effects:** Stratified partitioning ensures balanced distributions. Separate evaluation by query-length stratum confirms whether gains are uniform.

**Non-stationarity:** Seasonal and workload variation are represented in each split through calendar-quarter stratification, so both models are compared under similarly heterogeneous operating conditions rather than being advantaged or penalized by a particular evaluation window. Calendar-quarter subgroup analyses will still be reported to show whether performance is consistent across periods of the year.

**Physician population:** The experiment assumes stable physician population and information needs over 24 months. If significant turnover occurs, results should be stratified by department or role in sensitivity analysis.

### 8. Claims and Scope

If the neural model achieves statistically significantly higher query-level AUROC on expert-labeled test queries, the experiment establishes that fine-tuning on click-through and dwell-time logs produces better separation of relevant from non-relevant clinical documents than BM25 on this portal, for this physician population, over this time period. The experiment does *not* establish: causality (correlation between training on clicks and improved ranking), generalization to other hospitals or portals (external validity is limited), or that the neural model is superior to other retrieval approaches (e.g., hybrid methods, learned-to-rank systems). A live A/B test would be required to confirm that offline improvements translate to reduced query reformulation and search time in practice.

planted_issues.description: A split stratified across both calendar quarters and query lengths allows mixing of future observations into training data, violating the fundamental temporal structure required for clinical retrieval. This leaks future portal content and physician behavior patterns into the training set, invalidating generalization to future queries and inflating performance estimates beyond real-world deployment conditions.

Query-level AUROC measures overall discriminative power across all document pairs but fails to capture the critical top-k ranking objective of clinical retrieval, where physicians typically scan only the first 10 results. A model can achieve high AUROC by correctly separating many irrelevant documents in lower ranks while failing to place truly relevant documents in the most critical top positions.

must_not_claim.claim: The neural model and BM25 baseline should include document metadata features (type, recency, prominence) to better align with physician document selection patterns
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including metadata would confound semantic retrieval evaluation with metadata-driven shortcuts (e.g., recent documents appearing more relevant). The experiment specifically tests semantic matching capability by excluding metadata features to ensure gains are from improved language understanding rather than metadata artifacts.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic must identify that calendar-quarter stratification invalidates temporal validation by allowing future content exposure. This fundamentally undermines the experiment's ability to estimate deployment performance, as the temporal evolution of clinical content/guidelines requires chronological splitting to prevent leakage of future knowledge into training.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic explains how temporal leakage from calendar-quarter stratification enables models to overfit to future content and query patterns unavailable during deployment
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: Null - the temporal leakage in split method has no legitimate defense given the domain's strong requirement for chronological validation

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic must demonstrate that AUROC fails to capture the top-k operational requirement of clinicians scanning first 10 results. This renders the primary metric insensitive to critical failures in placing highly relevant documents at the top, despite its mathematical properties on full candidate sets.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic provides hypothetical results showing high AUROC with no relevant documents in top 10, coupled with domain context about time-sensitive clinical decisions requiring immediate relevance
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender might argue AUROC measures overall separation quality, but this argument fails given the experiment's explicit hypothesis about top-10 performance and stakeholder focus on fast access to correct guidance

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Neural Dual-Encoder vs. BM25 for Clinical Retrieval

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 24 months of production query logs from the hospital knowledge portal. Each interaction record contains a timestamped physician query, the set of documents presented to the physician, which document(s) were clicked, dwell time on clicked documents, and metadata about the document (type, creation/update date). All data are pseudonymized and conform to institutional data governance policies.

Labels for training are inferred from implicit feedback: a click on a document is treated as a positive signal; absence of a click is a negative. Dwell time (>30 seconds) serves as a secondary confirmation of relevance, though the primary training signal is the click itself. However, because click logs are biased by position and document prominence, we use expert annotation for all evaluation. A panel of five clinicians (emergency medicine, internal medicine, surgery) will independently annotate 500 randomly sampled query-document pairs sampled from the test period. Pairs are annotated on a three-point scale (not relevant, somewhat relevant, highly relevant) and blinded to retriever identity. Inter-rater agreement (Fleiss' kappa) is computed; disagreements are resolved by majority vote. These expert labels are used only for evaluation, never for model training, ensuring an unbiased assessment of true retrieval quality.

### 2. Data Splits and Temporal Strategy

Data are split chronologically: months 1–14 for training (70% of interactions), months 15–17 for validation (15%), and months 18–24 for held-out test (15%). A chronological split is essential because: (1) the portal's document collection and clinical guidelines evolve over time; random splitting would leak future documents and guidelines into the training set, inflating performance estimates; (2) physician query patterns change seasonally and in response to clinical updates, so temporal stratification reflects realistic deployment conditions; (3) the hypothesis is about future performance, so the test set must represent queries unseen during training.

Within each split, queries are stratified by length (short: <5 tokens, medium: 5–15 tokens, long: >15 tokens) to ensure balanced representation across splits. This prevents either model from exploiting query-length artifacts as a shortcut. Query length effects are monitored as a secondary metric to ensure gains are not driven by better handling of a single query type.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

No hand-engineered features are constructed. For the neural dual-encoder, raw query and document text (title + body concatenated) are tokenized and embedded via learned representations. Document metadata (type, recency, update frequency, prominence in the portal) are available but deliberately excluded from both the neural model and BM25 baseline, to isolate semantic retrieval quality and prevent gains from being driven by metadata shortcuts (e.g., preferring recently updated documents).

All preprocessing steps (tokenization, lowercasing, stop-word removal, punctuation normalization) are fit on training data only. The fitted tokenizer and vocabulary are then applied identically to validation and test data; no statistics are recomputed on held-out sets. For BM25, standard TF-IDF weighting with no query expansion is used. Hyperparameters are set to widely accepted defaults (k1=1.5, b=0.75) to ensure fair comparison and to avoid the appearance that the baseline was deliberately weakened through poor tuning.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a dual-encoder transformer (candidate: DistilBERT or a clinically adapted BERT checkpoint). The encoder processes queries and documents separately into fixed-size embedding vectors; retrieval is performed via nearest-neighbor search in embedding space (cosine similarity or a learned similarity function). Training uses a contrastive loss (InfoNCE or margin-based ranking loss) on clicked query-document pairs, with in-batch negatives and hard negatives mined from the training set during training.

The baseline is BM25, a standard lexical retrieval method, given identical preprocessing and no tuning effort beyond standard parameter settings. BM25 is an appropriate baseline because it is the current production system (or the simplest strong alternative) and represents the status quo; the hypothesis claims the neural model will exceed it, not merely that neural retrieval is feasible.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** nDCG@10 (normalized discounted cumulative gain at rank 10), computed on expert-labeled ground truth from the test set. nDCG@10 is chosen because: (1) physicians typically scan the first 10 results before reformulating queries, making top-10 performance clinically relevant; (2) the metric rewards ranking truly relevant documents higher, directly supporting the stakeholder goal of faster access to correct clinical guidance; (3) nDCG handles graded relevance (the 3-point expert labels) and is robust to sparse labels.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall@10: fraction of relevant documents retrieved in the top 10.
- MRR@10: position of the first relevant result, capturing whether top results are useful.
- nDCG@10 stratified by query length: reveals whether gains are uniform or concentrated in certain query types (e.g., the neural model might excel on long queries but underperform on short ones).
- In a live deployment phase, click-through rate and dwell time on retrieved documents are monitored to validate that offline nDCG improvements translate to changed physician behavior.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 15–17). A subset of 200 validation queries are expert-annotated (by the same panel, blind to condition). The primary metric for model selection is nDCG@10 on these expert-annotated validation queries. Early stopping and hyperparameter sweeps (embedding dimension, learning rate, batch size, contrastive loss temperature) are guided by validation nDCG@10. The model checkpoint with the highest validation nDCG@10 is selected and evaluated exactly once on the locked test set. The test set is not inspected during training or tuning; no intermediate results inform model selection.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope

**Position bias:** Clicks are biased toward the top-ranked document regardless of true relevance. This is controlled by using expert labels for evaluation (not clicks). During training, hard-negative mining—deliberately selecting non-clicked high-relevance documents as negatives—helps the model learn to distinguish truly relevant from merely prominent documents.

**Document popularity and freshness:** Both models are given identical features (raw text only), preventing the neural model from exploiting document recency or popularity. If the neural model outperforms BM25, the gain cannot be attributed to leveraging metadata shortcuts.

**Query-length effects:** Stratified splitting ensures balanced distributions. Separate evaluation by query-length stratum confirms whether gains are uniform.

**Non-stationarity:** The chronological split ensures the test set is temporally aligned with deployment conditions. Seasonal effects are monitored post-hoc (e.g., comparing metrics by calendar quarter) but are not controlled experimentally, as they reflect real-world deployment dynamics.

**Physician population:** The experiment assumes stable physician population and information needs over 24 months. If significant turnover occurs, results should be stratified by department or role in sensitivity analysis.

### 8. Claims and Scope

If the neural model achieves statistically significantly higher nDCG@10 on expert-labeled test queries, the experiment establishes that fine-tuning on click-through and dwell-time logs produces semantically better rankings than BM25 on this portal, for this physician population, over this time period. The experiment does *not* establish: causality (correlation between training on clicks and improved ranking), generalization to other hospitals or portals (external validity is limited), or that the neural model is superior to other retrieval approaches (e.g., hybrid methods, learned-to-rank systems). A live A/B test would be required to confirm that the offline improvements translate to reduced query reformulation and search time in practice.
difficulty_justification: Both corruptions introduce subtle but critical flaws: temporal leakage through calendar-quarter stratification requires domain-specific understanding of clinical data evolution, while the AUROC metric mismatch demands recognition of the operational requirement for top-k precision in time-sensitive medical decision-making despite AUROC's surface-level mathematical legitimacy.

hypothesis: For enterprise email security, a transformer-based classifier trained on subject line, sender features, and email body text will detect phishing and business-email-compromise messages more accurately than a rules-plus-gradient-boosted baseline, especially on previously unseen attack campaigns, as measured by area under the precision-recall curve.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity email defense in a large organization’s inbound mail gateway

## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Phishing Detection vs. Rules + Gradient Boosting

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling
This experiment uses 18 months of production email gateway logs (January 2022–June 2023) from the enterprise inbound mail system. The dataset includes raw email headers (sender, recipient, timestamp), subject lines, body text, attachment metadata, and URL/file reputation signals. Labels are obtained from post-hoc analyst adjudication, user-reported incidents, and sandbox URL detonation outcomes. A binary label (malicious/benign) is assigned: malicious includes phishing, business-email-compromise, credential harvesting, and confirmed social-engineering attacks; benign includes emails cleared or unmatched to security incidents. Emails without final adjudication are excluded. To quantify label quality, a random audit of 500 emails is independently re-reviewed by a second analyst; inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa) must exceed 0.85 to proceed.

### 2. Campaign-Aware Temporal Split
The data is split using a two-stage strategy to handle non-stationarity and campaign structure:
- **Temporal boundary:** Months 1–12 constitute the training set, months 13–15 the validation set, and months 16–18 the held-out test set. This chronological ordering prevents the model from training on attack patterns or campaign signatures that emerge after the test period.
- **Campaign stratification:** Each email is assigned to a campaign via clustering on sender domain, subject similarity (edit distance), URL/attachment hash, and analyst-provided campaign tags. Critically, no campaign is split across train/validation/test boundaries. All emails from the same phishing family remain in a single fold. This ensures evaluation reflects generalization to entirely new attack campaigns, not held-out variants of known families.
- **Justification:** Phishing and BEC attacks evolve over time; random splits would inflate performance by allowing the model to learn campaign-specific patterns that do not transfer to novel threats. Temporal + campaign-aware splits isolate the key operational requirement: detecting unseen attack types.

### 3. Feature Engineering
Both the transformer and baseline receive identical features:
- **Text features:** Subject line and body text are tokenized using a shared vocabulary and capped at 512 tokens. Preprocessing (lowercasing, special character handling, URL/email anonymization) is applied deterministically and fit on the training set only. Vocabulary is built on training text only; rare tokens (<5 occurrences) are marked [UNK].
- **Sender features:** Sender domain reputation (age, SPF/DKIM/DMARC alignment, prior malicious message ratio computed over the full 18-month observation window to stabilize estimates for low-volume domains), authentication pass rates (8 dimensions total).
- **Email metadata:** Attachment presence and MIME types, URL count, reply-to domain alignment, header anomalies (From/Return-Path mismatch).
- **Exclusions:** Analyst-added campaign IDs, notes, or metadata tags used during review are explicitly excluded to prevent label leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline
**Transformer model:** A BERT-style encoder (pretrained on general English or domain-specific email corpora) is fine-tuned on the training set. Input: concatenated subject + body text (max 512 tokens) + sender domain embedding + metadata features. Output: binary logits via 2-class softmax. The architecture is fixed at the published baseline (12 layers, 768 hidden dimensions, 12 attention heads) to isolate architectural advantage from tuning variance.

**Baseline:** A gradient boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost, LightGBM, or CatBoost) trained on the same features: TF-IDF vectorization of subject + body (max 5000 features, min document frequency 2), sender features (8 dimensions), and metadata. Both models receive equal hyperparameter tuning effort (same wall-clock time budget, grid or Bayesian search). The baseline is feature-matched and tuned with care; performance parity or transformer outperformance constitutes evidence of architectural benefit.

### 5. Model Selection and Validation
Hyperparameter tuning is performed via 5-fold temporal cross-validation within the training set (Jan–Apr, May–Aug, Sep–Dec 2022). The validation set (months 13–15) is used to select the best epoch/boosting round and confirm generalization. The test set is held out and touched exactly once after all tuning is complete.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). PR-AUC is chosen because: (1) it reflects the precision-recall tradeoff directly relevant to security operations (detecting attacks while minimizing false positives); (2) it is robust to class imbalance and independent of negative class frequency; (3) it is more informative than ROC-AUC in highly imbalanced settings. AUPRC is computed on the full test set.

**Secondary metrics:** (1) Precision and recall at the operating point selected by the security team (e.g., 95% recall). (2) AUPRC restricted to held-out new campaigns (campaigns with <10 training examples or zero training examples) to measure generalization to novel threats specifically. (3) Expected calibration error (ECE) on the validation set to ensure predicted probabilities are reliable for operational thresholding. (4) Stratified analysis: performance by campaign novelty (seen vs. unseen), sender volume quartile, and analyst confidence score.

### 7. Confound Control
The design addresses known confounds:
- **Feature richness:** Both models use identical features; differences arise from architecture, not data access.
- **Label leakage:** Analyst metadata is excluded from features before modeling.
- **Campaign leakage:** Chronological + campaign-aware splits prevent training on future signatures or campaign duplicates.
- **Duplicates:** Near-duplicate emails are identified (body text, sender, subject within 24 hours) before splitting; duplicates are retained within their campaign group but flagged to measure performance on truly unique messages.
- **Sanity checks:** Baseline with shuffled features must perform near random chance; if not, a latent confound is present. Test performance is stratified by campaign novelty, volume, and label confidence to identify whether gains concentrate in specific regimes (a sign of overfitting to common patterns rather than improved detection).

### 8. Claims Scope
This experiment establishes that a fine-tuned transformer architecture, given equivalent feature engineering and tuning effort as a gradient boosted baseline, achieves higher AUPRC on held-out temporal and campaign-stratified test data. It does not establish that transformers are superior for all security tasks, nor does it quantify the practical impact on security operations (e.g., reduction in user-reported incidents). Deployment impact would require A/B testing in production.

planted_issues.description: The sender domain's 'prior malicious message ratio' is calculated using data from the full 18-month observation window, including validation and test set periods. This introduces temporal leakage by incorporating future label information into a feature used for training and evaluation, inflating performance metrics and violating the experiment’s core requirement to generalize to unseen attack campaigns.

must_not_claim.claim: The campaign-aware temporal split is insufficient because the validation/test sets could still contain campaigns partially seen in training
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design explicitly groups all emails from a campaign into single folds using analyst-provided tags and clustering, ensuring the evaluation measures generalization to entirely new campaigns as intended.

must_not_claim.claim: TF-IDF for the baseline model creates a vocabulary using test set data
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The structured choices confirm that all preprocessing transformers (including vocabulary creation) are fit solely on the training set data, matching best practices for temporal splits.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates that the sender domain features in the corrupted design leak future label information by showing the prior malicious message ratio calculation includes post-training data, invalidating the temporal split and overestimating model generalization ability
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic provides evidence (e.g., data flow diagrams, pseudocode analysis) that sender domain statistics used during feature engineering depend on label information from validation/test periods

- **Sender features:** Sender domain reputation (age, SPF/DKIM/DMARC alignment, prior malicious message ratio from training set), authentication pass rates (8 dimensions total).
- **Email metadata:** Attachment presence and MIME types, URL count, reply-to domain alignment, header anomalies (From/Return-Path mismatch).
- **Exclusions:** Analyst-added campaign IDs, notes, or metadata tags used during review are explicitly excluded to prevent label leakage.

The validation set (months 13–15) is used to select the best epoch/boosting round and confirm generalization. The test set is held out and touched exactly once after all tuning is complete.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). PR-AUC is chosen because: (1) it reflects the precision-recall tradeoff directly relevant to security operations (detecting attacks while minimizing false positives); (2) it is robust to class imbalance and independent of negative class frequency; (3) it is more informative than ROC-AUC in highly imbalanced settings. AUPRC is computed on the full test set.

**Secondary metrics:** (1) Precision and recall at the operating point selected by the security team (e.g., 95% recall). (2) AUPRC restricted to held-out new campaigns (campaigns with <10 training examples or zero training examples) to measure generalization to novel threats specifically. (3) Expected calibration error (ECE) on the validation set to ensure predicted probabilities are reliable for operational thresholding. (4) Stratified analysis: performance by campaign novelty (seen vs. unseen), sender volume quartile, and analyst confidence score.

### 7. Confound Control

- **Feature richness:** Both models use identical features; differences arise from architecture, not data access.
- **Label leakage:** Analyst metadata is excluded from features before modeling.
- **Campaign leakage:** Chronological + campaign-aware splits prevent training on future signatures or campaign duplicates.
- **Duplicates:** Near-duplicate emails are identified (body text, sender, subject within 24 hours) before splitting; duplicates are retained within their campaign group but flagged to measure performance on truly unique messages.

difficulty_justification: This case requires domain-specific knowledge to identify that stabilizing domain statistics through full-period computation violates temporal constraints, despite appearing methodologically sound at first glance. The design’s legitimate use of external reputation databases creates a plausible cover for the corruption, making this a high-difficulty subtle leakage pattern relevant to real-world deployment scenarios.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer model that jointly encodes email subject/body text with sender-recipient graph features will detect malicious phishing emails more accurately than a text-only classifier, as measured by PR-AUC on temporally held-out mail traffic.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity email defense in a large organization’s inbound mail gateway
task_prompt:
### Experiment Design: Transformer with Joint Text and Graph Features for Enterprise Email Phishing Detection

**Objective:** Test whether a transformer model that jointly encodes email text (subject, body) and sender-recipient graph features detects phishing more accurately than a text-only transformer baseline, using ROC-AUC on held-out email traffic as the primary evaluation metric.

#### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy
The experiment uses 18 months of inbound email logs from the enterprise mail gateway, including subject lines, body text, MIME headers, sender domains, recipient lists, and attachment metadata. Labels are derived from security analyst verdicts (case closures, confirmed malicious determinations) and user-reported phishing submissions, with a conservative labeling rule: an email is labeled positive (malicious) only if it receives ≥2 independent confirmations or analyst closure as confirmed phishing. All other mail (legitimate, unconfirmed, or policy-based blocks) is labeled negative. This conservative approach minimizes label noise from false positives while acknowledging that some true positives may be unlabeled; the impact is reduced sensitivity but more reliable training signal.

#### 2. Data Split: Stratified Corpus Partitioning Strategy
To keep the malicious/benign ratio and sender-domain rarity profile comparable across development stages, the 18-month corpus will be partitioned using stratified sampling at the message level rather than contiguous calendar blocks.
- **Training set:** 70% of messages.
- **Validation set:** 15% of messages (hyperparameter tuning and model selection).
- **Test set:** 15% of messages (held-out evaluation).

The partitioning procedure will preserve overall label prevalence and coarse month-of-arrival composition within each split, while also balancing rare vs. frequent sender domains so subgroup estimates remain stable. This design reduces sensitivity to isolated campaign bursts or quarter-specific reporting artifacts that could otherwise dominate one contiguous holdout window and lead to brittle model-selection decisions.

#### 3. Feature Engineering
**Text features:** Subject and body text are tokenized using a vocabulary of 5,000 most-frequent tokens, with out-of-vocabulary tokens mapped to [UNK]. Auxiliary numeric features include raw text length, character density (digits, punctuation), and binary indicators for common phishing keywords ("verify," "urgent," "confirm password," etc.). To keep token cutoffs and scaling behavior stable across partitions, the preprocessing specification will be fixed once during corpus preparation using the unlabeled message pool and then reused unchanged throughout model development.

**Graph features:** A sender-recipient domain graph is constructed from the development corpus. Four features are computed per email: (1) sender domain in-degree (how many distinct recipients has this sender contacted?), (2) sender out-degree (how many distinct senders has the recipient received from?), (3) sender reputation (fraction of prior sender emails flagged as malicious), and (4) binary first-time sender flag (domain not observed in the established graph). Graph statistics are frozen once the feature-generation pass is complete; no graph updates occur during model fitting or final evaluation, which keeps the structural representation consistent across experiments.

All numeric features (text statistics and graph features) are standardized using the fixed preprocessing configuration; the same transformation is applied during training, validation, and test evaluation.

#### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline
**Proposed model (text + graph):** A two-stream architecture: (a) a transformer text encoder (BERT or DistilBERT, pretrained, fine-tuned on training set) outputs a [CLS] embedding (~768 dim); (b) a small feedforward network (2 layers, 128 units, ReLU) encodes the 4 graph features into a 64-dim embedding; (c) embeddings are concatenated and passed through a 2-layer classifier (256→64 units, ReLU, sigmoid output). The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and class weighting (weight_positive = N_neg / N_pos) to handle imbalance.

**Baseline (text-only):** A transformer text encoder from the same pretrained family receives the same text inputs and auxiliary numeric text statistics but no graph features. A single classification head produces binary predictions. To keep the baseline focused on the value of text alone rather than on optimization breadth, it will use one standard fine-tuning configuration with fixed learning rate and dropout, while the joint model receives the full hyperparameter search. Both models are still trained on identical data and evaluated under the same inference protocol.

#### 5. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Receiver Operating Characteristic AUC (ROC-AUC) on the held-out test set. ROC-AUC is chosen because it evaluates score ordering quality across the full operating range rather than at a single alert threshold, which matches the core scientific question of whether graph context improves separability beyond text alone. It also allows comparison of the joint and text-only models without tying conclusions to one organization-specific threshold policy.

**Secondary metrics:** (1) F1 score at the validation-optimized threshold; (2) False Positive Rate at 80% recall (operational question: can we catch 80% of attacks with <2% false alerts?); (3) Precision at 90% recall; (4) Stratified ROC-AUC on test subgroups: new sender domains, frequent sender domains, and temporal drift slices. Stratified metrics detect whether improvements generalize or reflect memorization, while the thresholded secondary metrics anchor the ranking results to practical alerting behavior.

#### 6. Model Selection and Validation
Both models are trained on the training set with multiple random seeds and learning rate schedules where applicable. At each epoch, both are evaluated on the validation set; the checkpoint maximizing validation ROC-AUC is retained for each model. After this validation-stage selection, the held-out test partition will be used for a final stability check before the model is locked for reporting. If the top validation checkpoint and a nearby alternative are effectively tied on validation, the final representative checkpoint will be the one with the more stable month-slice operating profile on the held-out partition, particularly lower variation in false-positive burden. This is intended to reduce the chance of selecting a checkpoint whose apparent gain is specific to the validation window alone.

#### 7. Confound Controls and Interpretation Scope
The experiment controls for sender reputation memorization by evaluating performance separately on new vs. known sender domains. If the joint model's advantage shrinks on new domains, this indicates the model may be exploiting sender history rather than learning semantic phishing cues. Graph features are frozen after feature construction, preventing moving-target effects during model comparison. Post-hoc ablation (removing graph features one at a time) reveals which signals drive the predicted improvement.

**What the experiment establishes:** Whether joint text + graph modeling outperforms text-only modeling on held-out enterprise email traffic, with thresholded secondary analyses reflecting low-false-positive operating constraints.

**What it does not establish:** Causality (why the joint model performs better, whether semantic content or sender history is primary), generalization to other organizations (domain adaptation), or real-world deployment feasibility (alert response latency, false-positive cost tolerance). These require follow-up studies.

planted_issues.description: The stratified message-level split allows future sender behavior and campaign bursts to influence training data patterns, violating temporal validity. This enables the model to learn from future data, leading to unrealistically optimistic results.
Preprocessing statistics like tokenization and feature scaling are computed using the full dataset, including test data. This leaks future information into training, biasing performance estimates.

ROC-AUC as a primary metric is inappropriate for the severely imbalanced phishing detection task. It rewards high true-negative accuracy, conflicting with the low false-positive operational requirement.
The text-only baseline receives less hyperparameter tuning, conflating model architecture benefits with optimization effort, thus inflating the apparent value of graph features.
The test set is used to select the most stable checkpoint, invalidating its role as an unbiased estimate of generalization performance by feeding results back into model choice.

must_not_claim.claim: A stratified split is necessary to ensure balanced class representations across partitions.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Email traffic evolves temporally, and message-level stratification introduces leakage from future campaigns into training data. The correct temporal split ensures forward-in-time evaluation realism.

must_not_claim.claim: Using the full dataset to fix preprocessing parameters improves stability of token cutoffs and scaling.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Preprocessing must be fit solely on training data to avoid leakage. Using the full dataset biases results by allowing future data to influence training representations.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic exposes temporal leakage and demonstrates that the stratified split enables future data exploitation, invalidating generalization claims.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Evidence shows the split allows future email patterns to influence training, directly undermining the evaluation's temporal validity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies that ROC-AUC's insensitivity to imbalance leads to misleading performance assessments incompatible with the low false-positive requirement.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Analysis confirms ROC-AUC rewards high true-negative rates irrelevant to the operational constraint of minimizing false alerts.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic reveals test set contamination invalidates the final stability check by feeding evaluation results back into model selection.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Documentation confirms test data influenced checkpoint choice, breaking the independence of final evaluation.

sound_design_reference:
### Experiment Design: Transformer with Joint Text and Graph Features for Enterprise Email Phishing Detection

**Objective:** Test whether a transformer model that jointly encodes email text (subject, body) and sender-recipient graph features detects phishing more accurately than a text-only transformer baseline, using PR-AUC on temporally held-out email traffic as the primary evaluation metric.

#### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy
The experiment uses 18 months of inbound email logs from the enterprise mail gateway, including subject lines, body text, MIME headers, sender domains, recipient lists, and attachment metadata. Labels are derived from security analyst verdicts (case closures, confirmed malicious determinations) and user-reported phishing submissions, with a conservative labeling rule: an email is labeled positive (malicious) only if it receives ≥2 independent confirmations or analyst closure as confirmed phishing. All other mail (legitimate, unconfirmed, or policy-based blocks) is labeled negative. This conservative approach minimizes label noise from false positives while acknowledging that some true positives may be unlabeled; the impact is reduced sensitivity but more reliable training signal.

#### 2. Data Split: Temporal Chronological Strategy
To prevent leakage and align with realistic deployment, the data is split strictly by time: 
- **Training set:** Months 1–12 (establish baseline sender reputation and attack patterns).
- **Validation set:** Months 13–15 (hyperparameter tuning and model selection; 3 months provides stability and temporal separation from training).
- **Test set:** Months 16–18 (held-out future traffic; represents new campaigns and evolved attack tactics).

No random shuffling is applied. This temporal split prevents the model from exploiting future information (e.g., knowing which senders later become malicious) and ensures evaluation is on genuinely unseen data. The 3-month test window is sufficient to detect real-world performance degradation and seasonal drift.

#### 3. Feature Engineering
**Text features:** Subject and body text are tokenized using a vocabulary of 5,000 most-frequent tokens (fit on training set only), with out-of-vocabulary tokens mapped to [UNK]. Auxiliary numeric features include raw text length, character density (digits, punctuation), and binary indicators for common phishing keywords ("verify," "urgent," "confirm password," etc.).

**Graph features:** A sender-recipient domain graph is constructed from training-set messages only. Four features are computed per email: (1) sender domain in-degree (how many distinct recipients has this sender contacted in training?), (2) sender out-degree (how many distinct senders has the recipient received from?), (3) sender reputation (fraction of prior sender emails flagged as malicious in training), and (4) binary first-time sender flag (domain not seen in training). Graph statistics are frozen at the end of month 12; no graph updates occur during validation or testing, preventing information leakage from future emails.

All numeric features (text statistics and graph features) are standardized using training-set mean and standard deviation; validation and test sets are transformed using training-set statistics only.

#### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline
**Proposed model (text + graph):** A two-stream architecture: (a) a transformer text encoder (BERT or DistilBERT, pretrained, fine-tuned on training set) outputs a [CLS] embedding (~768 dim); (b) a small feedforward network (2 layers, 128 units, ReLU) encodes the 4 graph features into a 64-dim embedding; (c) embeddings are concatenated and passed through a 2-layer classifier (256→64 units, ReLU, sigmoid output). The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and class weighting (weight_positive = N_neg / N_pos) to handle imbalance.

**Baseline (text-only):** An identical transformer text encoder architecture with the same fine-tuning procedure, but receiving only text features and auxiliary numeric text statistics (no graph features). A single classification head produces binary predictions. The baseline receives the same hyperparameter tuning effort, computational budget, and training procedure (same epochs, class weighting, learning rate schedules) as the proposed model, ensuring a fair comparison.

#### 5. Evaluation Metrics
**Primary metric:** Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC) on the held-out test set (months 16–18). PR-AUC is chosen because (1) the task is highly imbalanced (malicious emails are rare), making PR-AUC more informative than ROC-AUC; (2) the operational objective is to maximize true positives (detected phishing) at a constrained false-positive rate (alert budget), which PR-AUC directly captures; (3) PR-AUC is robust to class imbalance and does not reward high true-negative accuracy.

**Secondary metrics:** (1) F1 score at the validation-optimized threshold; (2) False Positive Rate at 80% recall (operational question: can we catch 80% of attacks with <2% false alerts?); (3) Precision at 90% recall; (4) Stratified PR-AUC on test subgroups: new sender domains, frequent sender domains, and temporal drift (month 16–17 vs. 18). Stratified metrics detect whether improvements generalize or reflect memorization.

#### 6. Model Selection and Validation
Both models are trained on the training set with multiple random seeds and learning rate schedules. At each epoch, both are evaluated on the validation set (months 13–15); the checkpoint maximizing validation PR-AUC is retained for each model. Once model selection is complete, the selected checkpoints are evaluated on the test set (months 16–18) exactly once. No test-set information is used during training or hyperparameter search.

#### 7. Confound Controls and Interpretation Scope
The experiment controls for sender reputation memorization by evaluating performance separately on new vs. known sender domains. If the joint model's advantage shrinks on new domains, this indicates the model may be exploiting sender history rather than learning semantic phishing cues. Graph features are frozen after training, preventing information leakage from future sender behavior. Post-hoc ablation (removing graph features one at a time) reveals which signals drive the predicted improvement.

**What the experiment establishes:** Whether joint text + graph modeling outperforms text-only modeling on future unseen email traffic, at a decision threshold optimized for the business constraint of low false positives.

**What it does not establish:** Causality (why the joint model performs better, whether semantic content or sender history is primary), generalization to other organizations (domain adaptation), or real-world deployment feasibility (alert response latency, false-positive cost tolerance). These require follow-up studies.
difficulty_justification: The case combines subtle preprocessing leakage, temporal leakage masked by stratified sampling, and evaluation contamination that cumulatively inflate performance estimates. Reviewers must understand domain-specific requirements (e.g., low false positives under imbalance) and the causal role of temporal splits to identify all flaws.

hypothesis: In a hospital laboratory operations setting, a gradient-boosted model that uses patient-level lab-order sequences, specimen collection timestamps, and prior turnaround-time history will predict delayed critical-result delivery better than a baseline using only static order and patient demographics, as measured on future weeks of lab activity.
domain: Hospital laboratory operations using electronic lab order, specimen tracking, and result-routing logs from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Predicting Lab Critical-Result Delivery Delays Using Sequence and Temporal Features

### 1. Data and Labeling

We would extract 36 months of operational lab activity (January 2021–December 2023) from the integrated LIS/EHR logs of a multi-hospital health system, capturing all lab orders, specimen timestamps, analyzer results, result verification times, and provider acknowledgment events. We expect 3–5 million lab orders across all hospitals and wards. The label is deterministically defined: a critical result is marked as **delayed** if the time from result verification to provider acknowledgment exceeds the lab-defined SLA (e.g., 60 minutes for critical cardiac markers). Labeling is objective and reproducible, derived directly from timestamped log entries; no manual review is performed.

### 2. Train–Validation–Test Split Strategy

Data is partitioned by **stratified sampling across the full 36-month window**, allocating approximately 67% to training, 16.5% to validation, and 16.5% to test.
- **Training set:** ~67% of orders
- **Validation set:** ~16.5% of orders
- **Test set:** ~16.5% of orders, held completely separate

We preserve the joint distribution of **hospital site and order criticality category** (STAT, Critical, Routine) in each split so that all hospitals and order types are represented and site-by-site performance evaluation remains stable. This partitioning is intended to prevent low-volume sites or rare critical-order categories from being underrepresented in validation or test, which is especially important because the primary operating point is defined at high recall and therefore depends on reliable estimation of the positive class across subgroups. Stratification also helps ensure that performance differences can be attributed to sequence modeling rather than evaluation artifacts driven by an unusually easy or difficult seasonal slice.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Preprocessing parameters are fixed once using the complete extracted cohort before model fitting so that scaling, categorical level handling, and imputation remain consistent across hospitals and low-volume order categories. Those frozen transformation parameters are then applied unchanged during training, validation, and test. This avoids split-specific preprocessing drift when some analyzers, wards, or order types appear only sparsely in a given partition.

Features are constructed in two groups:

**Sequence and historical features:**
- For each order, extract the prior 10 lab orders from the same patient in the preceding 30 days.
- Encode each prior order: criticality (STAT/Critical/Routine), result turnaround time (minutes), analyzer type, days since prior order.
- Aggregate across the sequence: mean turnaround time, maximum turnaround time, coefficient of variation, count of prior SLA misses (delays).
- Patient-level summary (180-day lookback): total orders, total delays, rolling delay rate (delays / total), mean turnaround time in the prior 7 days.

**Order-context and temporal features:**
- Order criticality, order type (panel vs. single analyte), assigned analyzer, destination ward acuity.
- Temporal features: hour of day, day of week, night-shift indicator (19:00–07:00), shift-change period.

**Feature construction uses only data up to the prediction timestamp** (specimen collection time); no post-collection, post-result, or post-acknowledgment information is used. All feature transformations are deterministic and reproducible.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model:** Gradient boosted trees (XGBoost or LightGBM). Justification: gradient boosting captures non-linear interactions (e.g., STAT orders with high prior delay rates are high-risk), handles categorical features naturally, and is interpretable for operational deployment via feature importance rankings.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression using only static features—order criticality, order type, analyzer assignment, destination ward, ordering service, and patient demographics (age, admission status). No sequence or turnaround-history features are included. This baseline represents a realistic operational alternative and tests whether sequence modeling and history are necessary.

Both models are tuned with equal effort: hyperparameters (L2 regularization for logistic regression; tree depth, learning rate, and sample weights for gradient boosting) are selected on the validation set using the primary metric. Both use identical preprocessing and train–validation–test splits. A single gradient-boosted model is trained on the full training set using the selected hyperparameters; no ensemble is used.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** **Precision at 90% recall** (or equivalently, False Positive Rate at 90% sensitivity). 

Justification: The operational objective is to catch as many true delays as possible (high recall) while keeping false alarms manageable. A 90% recall operating point reflects the lab's alert budget: ~10% of delayed cases may go unsignaled, but the remaining 90% are caught with acceptable false-alarm cost. Precision directly measures false positives (nursing/lab coordination overhead). This metric is robust to the expected class imbalance (<5% of orders are truly delayed) and avoids threshold-selection bias by pre-specifying the recall target.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Area under the Precision–Recall curve (AUPRC): summarizes performance across all operating points.
- Recall at 95% precision: high-confidence alerts only.
- Calibration curve and Brier score: verifies predicted probabilities are reliable for adaptive thresholding.
- Precision and recall stratified by hospital site and order criticality: detects site-specific or category-specific performance degradation.

### 6. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Hyperparameter search is conducted on the **validation set only**, using precision at 90% recall as the selection metric. The hyperparameter set maximizing validation precision at 90% recall is locked in. The test set is not touched during tuning. Once hyperparameters are selected, a single model is trained on the full training set using those hyperparameters.

### 7. Test Set Policy and Evaluation

The test set is held completely separate and used exactly once for final evaluation. A frozen, version-controlled copy is used to compute all reported metrics. 95% confidence intervals are computed via bootstrap resampling to quantify uncertainty. No model retraining, feature engineering revision, or metric re-selection occurs after test evaluation.

### 8. Controlling for Confounds

- **Site-level workflow differences:** Stratified metrics (precision and recall by hospital and ward) are computed to detect whether improvements are site-specific or systematic. If concentrated at certain sites, the model is interpreted accordingly or site is added as a covariate.
- **Temporal variation:** Including the full 36-month range in each split reduces the chance that evaluation is dominated by a single unusual operating period and allows threshold estimates to reflect the range of observed workflows.
- **Shift and workload effects:** Shift and workload-proxy features are included; feature importance is inspected to verify that sequence features drive the improvement, not shift alone.
- **Class imbalance:** Stratification during splits and use of precision–recall metrics (not accuracy) prevent trivial majority-class predictions.
- **Label leakage:** Labels are derived deterministically from timestamps only; no reclassification or chart review occurs.
- **Sanity check:** Predictions on the baseline (static-only) feature subset are inspected; divergence from baseline features flags overfitting.

### 9. Scope of Claims

This design will establish whether a gradient-boosted model using patient-level sequence and turnaround-time history achieves better precision at 90% recall than a static-features baseline on held-out lab activity. It does not establish causality (why delays occur) or whether the model generalizes to hospitals outside this health system. Site-stratified results will clarify whether the improvement is workflow-specific or systematic.

planted_issues.description: The stratified sampling across entire 36-month window allows future data to influence model development. This violates the prospective prediction requirement of evaluating future weeks and invalidates performance estimates under temporal drift.

Preprocessing parameters are fitted on the full cohort (including validation/test data), permitting hold-out distributional characteristics to influence training and inflating performance metrics through leakage.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of stratification by hospital site and criticality category might mask temporal confounding effects or underrepresent critical-delay patterns in specific periods.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratified sampling on hospital and criticality ensures subgroup representation, enabling stable evaluation of rare categories. However, temporal split is separately required to reflect the prospective deployment task and control for workflow drift over time.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies temporal leakage from the use of stratified sampling across periods, rendering performance estimates invalid under temporal drift. The defender cannot legitimate stratified sampling for this hypothesis, as temporal splits are essential for prospective deployment scenarios.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that the evaluation framework allows future data patterns to influence model training, violating the prediction task's requirement to predict unseen future activity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies preprocessing leakage through full-cohort parameter fitting, invalidating the independence of validation/test evaluations. The defender cannot justify this practice, as preprocessing fitted entirely on training data is necessary to avoid inflating metrics through distributional leakage.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic provides evidence that preprocessing parameters derived from validation/test data have skewed the training process, leading to overly optimistic performance estimates.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Predicting Lab Critical-Result Delivery Delays Using Sequence and Temporal Features

### 1. Data and Labeling

We would extract 36 months of operational lab activity (January 2021–December 2023) from the integrated LIS/EHR logs of a multi-hospital health system, capturing all lab orders, specimen timestamps, analyzer results, result verification times, and provider acknowledgment events. We expect 3–5 million lab orders across all hospitals and wards. The label is deterministically defined: a critical result is marked as **delayed** if the time from result verification to provider acknowledgment exceeds the lab-defined SLA (e.g., 60 minutes for critical cardiac markers). Labeling is objective and reproducible, derived directly from timestamped log entries; no manual review is performed.

### 2. Train–Validation–Test Split Strategy

Data is split **temporally and chronologically** to prevent future-information leakage and reflect the operational prediction task:
- **Training set:** Months 1–24 (first 24 months of data)
- **Validation set:** Months 25–30 (following 6 months)
- **Test set:** Months 31–36 (final 6 months, held completely separate)

Within each set, we **stratify by hospital site and order criticality category** (STAT, Critical, Routine) to ensure all hospitals and order types are represented and to enable site-by-site performance evaluation. Temporal splits are necessary because: (1) the deployment scenario requires predicting on *future* weeks unseen during training; (2) lab workflows, staffing, and equipment evolve, introducing temporal drift; and (3) any post-result information (e.g., later acknowledgments) must be excluded from the prediction timestamp. Stratification ensures we can detect whether improvements are driven by sequence modeling or by site-specific workflow shortcuts.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing steps (scaling, encoding, imputation) are **fit exclusively on the training set** and applied without refitting to validation and test. This prevents leakage of validation/test information into preprocessing parameters.

Features are constructed in two groups:

**Sequence and historical features:**
- For each order, extract the prior 10 lab orders from the same patient in the preceding 30 days.
- Encode each prior order: criticality (STAT/Critical/Routine), result turnaround time (minutes), analyzer type, days since prior order.
- Aggregate across the sequence: mean turnaround time, maximum turnaround time, coefficient of variation, count of prior SLA misses (delays).
- Patient-level summary (180-day lookback): total orders, total delays, rolling delay rate (delays / total), mean turnaround time in the prior 7 days.

**Order-context and temporal features:**
- Order criticality, order type (panel vs. single analyte), assigned analyzer, destination ward acuity.
- Temporal features: hour of day, day of week, night-shift indicator (19:00–07:00), shift-change period.

**Feature construction uses only data up to the prediction timestamp** (specimen collection time); no post-collection, post-result, or post-acknowledgment information is used. All feature transformations are deterministic and reproducible.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Model:** Gradient boosted trees (XGBoost or LightGBM). Justification: gradient boosting captures non-linear interactions (e.g., STAT orders with high prior delay rates are high-risk), handles categorical features naturally, and is interpretable for operational deployment via feature importance rankings.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression using only static features—order criticality, order type, analyzer assignment, destination ward, ordering service, and patient demographics (age, admission status). No sequence or turnaround-history features are included. This baseline represents a realistic operational alternative and tests whether sequence modeling and history are necessary.

Both models are tuned with equal effort: hyperparameters (L2 regularization for logistic regression; tree depth, learning rate, and sample weights for gradient boosting) are selected on the validation set using the primary metric. Both use identical preprocessing and train–validation–test splits. A single gradient-boosted model is trained on the full training set using the selected hyperparameters; no ensemble is used.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** **Precision at 90% recall** (or equivalently, False Positive Rate at 90% sensitivity). 

Justification: The operational objective is to catch as many true delays as possible (high recall) while keeping false alarms manageable. A 90% recall operating point reflects the lab's alert budget: ~10% of delayed cases may go unsignaled, but the remaining 90% are caught with acceptable false-alarm cost. Precision directly measures false positives (nursing/lab coordination overhead). This metric is robust to the expected class imbalance (<5% of orders are truly delayed) and avoids threshold-selection bias by pre-specifying the recall target.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Area under the Precision–Recall curve (AUPRC): summarizes performance across all operating points.
- Recall at 95% precision: high-confidence alerts only.
- Calibration curve and Brier score: verifies predicted probabilities are reliable for adaptive thresholding.
- Precision and recall stratified by hospital site and order criticality: detects site-specific or category-specific performance degradation.

### 6. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Hyperparameter search is conducted on the **validation set only** (months 25–30), using precision at 90% recall as the selection metric. The hyperparameter set maximizing validation precision at 90% recall is locked in. The test set is not touched during tuning. Once hyperparameters are selected, a single model is trained on the full training set using those hyperparameters.

### 7. Test Set Policy and Evaluation

The test set (months 31–36) is held completely separate and used exactly once for final evaluation. A frozen, version-controlled copy is used to compute all reported metrics. 95% confidence intervals are computed via bootstrap resampling to quantify uncertainty. No model retraining, feature engineering revision, or metric re-selection occurs after test evaluation.

### 8. Controlling for Confounds

- **Site-level workflow differences:** Stratified metrics (precision and recall by hospital and ward) are computed to detect whether improvements are site-specific or systematic. If concentrated at certain sites, the model is interpreted accordingly or site is added as a covariate.
- **Temporal drift:** Temporal split ensures the test set represents future conditions unseen during training.
- **Shift and workload effects:** Shift and workload-proxy features are included; feature importance is inspected to verify that sequence features drive the improvement, not shift alone.
- **Class imbalance:** Stratification during splits and use of precision–recall metrics (not accuracy) prevent trivial majority-class predictions.
- **Label leakage:** Labels are derived deterministically from timestamps only; no reclassification or chart review occurs.
- **Sanity check:** Predictions on the baseline (static-only) feature subset are inspected; divergence from baseline features flags overfitting.

### 9. Scope of Claims

This design will establish whether a gradient-boosted model using patient-level sequence and turnaround-time history achieves better precision at 90% recall than a static-features baseline on future lab activity (test set). It does not establish causality (why delays occur) or whether the model generalizes to hospitals outside this health system. Site-stratified results will clarify whether the improvement is workflow-specific or systematic.
difficulty_justification: The interaction between temporal and preprocessing leakage makes both issues more difficult to identify than in isolation. Reviewers must recognize that stratified sampling and preprocessing strategies violate the prospective task definition despite appearing methodologically sound.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity email defense, a transformer fine-tuned on full email text plus sender/recipient graph features will outperform a bag-of-words baseline on phishing and business-email-compromise detection, especially under a time-based holdout that simulates novel attack campaigns.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity email defense in a large organization’s inbound mail gateway
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. BoW for Enterprise Email Phishing Detection

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of inbound email records (months 1–18) from an enterprise mail gateway. This timespan is sufficient to capture evolving attack tactics, seasonal patterns, and real concept drift. Raw data includes email headers, full body text, sender/recipient pairs, URL and attachment metadata, and analyst-assigned security labels.

Labels are defined as follows: an email is marked **malicious** if an analyst confirmed a phishing or business-email-compromise incident within 7 days of receipt, or if a user reported the email as malicious and an analyst validated it. All other emails are marked **benign**. Labels are assigned retrospectively, so there is inherent delay and potential noise. To quantify this noise, a random stratified sample of ~200 emails will be re-reviewed by a second analyst; inter-rater agreement will be computed (Cohen's kappa) and reported. Disagreement rates >30% will be flagged as a data quality concern.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

A chronological split is essential because email threats are non-stationary: attacker tactics evolve, sender infrastructure changes, and organization-specific business context shifts over time. A random split would allow the model to see future campaigns during training, inflating performance and creating a misleading comparison.

**Train:** Months 1–12 (12 months)
**Validation:** Months 13–15 (3 months)
**Test:** Months 16–18 (3 months)

Within each temporal fold, the data is further stratified by label (malicious/benign) to preserve class distribution across splits. This protects against random imbalance while maintaining the temporal ordering.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Two feature groups are constructed:

**Text Features:** The full email body text is tokenized using a standard BERT tokenizer and embedded via a pretrained transformer. Minimal preprocessing—only HTML tag stripping and non-ASCII removal—is applied to preserve semantic nuance.

**Metadata Features:** Sender domain reputation (fraction of emails from this domain labeled malicious in the training set only), recipient count, URL count, attachment count, presence of executable attachments, and SPF/DKIM pass rate. All metadata features are scaled to zero mean and unit variance using statistics computed from the training set; the fitted scaler is applied identically to validation and test sets. No information from validation or test is used during preprocessing to prevent leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Proposed Model:** A BERT-like pretrained transformer is fine-tuned end-to-end on the training set. Metadata features are concatenated to the [CLS] token embedding, and the combined representation is passed through a 2-layer MLP head (hidden dimension 256, dropout 0.2) and sigmoid output for binary classification. Hyperparameters—learning rate, batch size, epochs, dropout, and weight decay—are tuned on the validation set via grid search over a fixed budget of 50 trials.

**Baseline:** Bag-of-words logistic regression. Email body text is vectorized using TF-IDF (fit on the training set only, max features = 10,000), then concatenated with the same metadata features. Hyperparameter tuning (regularization strength C) is performed on the validation set under the same budget and method as the transformer. This ensures a fair comparison: both models receive equivalent tuning effort, the same feature set, and are evaluated under identical conditions. The baseline isolates whether the transformer's semantic representation and fine-tuning provide genuine benefit over simpler text representation.

### 5. Evaluation Strategy

**Primary Metric:** Precision-Recall AUC (PR-AUC) on the final holdout portion of the test period. PR-AUC is chosen because (1) the email dataset is class-imbalanced (malicious emails are ~2–5% of traffic), and PR-AUC is more sensitive to minority-class performance than ROC-AUC; (2) in operations, the security team has limited capacity to triage alerts, so the ability to rank-order emails by risk and maintain high precision at a fixed recall level is operationally meaningful.

**Secondary Metrics:** (1) Precision at 90% Recall, representing the false positive rate when the team commits to reviewing 90% of malicious emails; (2) F1 score for overall balance; (3) calibration curve to assess whether the transformer's confidence scores are reliable for risk-based decision-making.

### 6. Controls for Confounds and Leakage

Several potential confounds are addressed:

- **Campaign leakage:** After training, the experiment identifies sender/recipient clusters and campaign-like patterns (e.g., emails with identical subject lines from the same sender). Any clusters appearing in both training and test sets are flagged, and the proportion of test predictions from leaked clusters is reported. If >5% of test emails belong to leaked clusters, the split is re-evaluated or sender-based stratification is applied.

- **Label noise:** A second analyst independently re-labels a stratified sample from both training and test sets. Inter-rater agreement (kappa) is computed; kappa < 0.70 triggers discussion of label quality limitations.

- **Sender reputation confound:** A metadata-only baseline (no text, only sender reputation and structural features) is trained on the same splits. If this model achieves >80% of the transformer's test PR-AUC, the conclusion is that sender reputation, not semantic understanding, drives performance.

- **Temporal drift:** Performance is evaluated separately for each month in the test period (months 16, 17, 18). Significant degradation in later months suggests the model does not generalize to truly novel campaigns.

- **Class balance drift:** The proportion of malicious emails is computed for each temporal fold. Divergence >5% is documented as a potential source of bias.

### 7. Final Validation and Reporting

Once hyperparameters are finalized using the validation set, the test period is partitioned into two random halves. The first half is used as a deployment-readiness confirmation pass to verify that the selected configuration remains stable under the final attack mix before the training recipe is locked. The second half is retained as the final holdout for reported metrics. All reported metrics (PR-AUC, precision@recall, F1, calibration) are computed on the second half. Results are aggregated across 3 random seeds with mean and 95% confidence intervals.

planted_issues.description: The test set is partially used during a deployment-readiness confirmation step to select the final model configuration. This invalidates the final holdout's independence for unbiased performance reporting, as the confirmation pass allows the model to fit to the test data distribution.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of stratification within the temporal test fold introduces data leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratification by label within a fixed time interval is a valid technique to preserve class distribution without violating temporal ordering. It is appropriate in non-stationary settings where class imbalance could otherwise distort evaluation.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer must identify that the deployment-readiness confirmation pass on part of the test set constitutes model selection using test data, invalidating the final holdout for unbiased reporting. The defender has no valid rebuttal to this critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer correctly argues that any model decision influenced by test data (even to confirm stability) compromises the final evaluation's objectivity.
