## Experiment Design: Temporal Graph Neural Networks for Enterprise Intrusion Detection

### 1. Objective
To test whether a temporal graph neural network that models host-to-host and user-to-host interactions over sliding time windows outperforms a tabular gradient-boosted baseline on incident-level detection performance, particularly for rare lateral-movement attacks in enterprise network data.

### 2. Data Strategy
The experiment uses 18 months of production data from a large enterprise environment, comprising network flow logs, endpoint process telemetry, authentication logs, and asset/user metadata. Labels are sourced from the enterprise's SIEM and case-management system, representing confirmed security incidents. To account for label delay (many incidents are confirmed during post-incident investigation), a (host, time_window) is labeled positive if the host was involved in a confirmed incident during that window or within 7 days following. This labeling window is fixed retroactively across all 18 months to avoid introducing future information during training. Approximately 5,000+ hosts and 10,000+ users are represented, with incident prevalence expected to be <0.5% at the daily host level (reflecting extreme class imbalance typical in security). Incidents are categorized by type (lateral movement, exfiltration, malware) for stratification and secondary analysis.

### 3. Temporal Split Strategy
A strict chronological split is used: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, and months 16–18 for held-out testing. This split is critical because (a) the deployment context requires predicting future incidents, so randomly interleaving training and test data would misrepresent real-world conditions; (b) temporal interaction patterns are central to the hypothesis, and random shuffling would destroy the signal; (c) temporal splits prevent leakage from post-incident artifacts (alerts, patches, isolation actions) that accumulate after detection. All preprocessing, feature construction, and model selection occur on the training and validation windows only; the test set is never touched until final evaluation.

### 4. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing
Features are constructed from raw signals: network flows (source/destination IPs, ports, protocols, bytes, duration, timing), endpoint events (processes, users, file/registry operations), authentication logs, and asset metadata (OS, department, criticality). For the tabular baseline, features include per-host traffic volume, destination IP diversity, port/protocol distributions, temporal patterns (off-hours activity), per-user login frequency, login failures, and per-asset centrality scores (degree, betweenness) computed on the full training period plus asset criticality. For the graph model, the same raw signals are used to construct dynamic interaction graphs, with nodes representing hosts/users and edges representing observed flows or authentications within 7-day sliding windows. Node features combine asset metadata and temporal positional encodings (hour of day, day of week, time-since-last-interaction). All statistical transformers (scalers, encoders, imputation strategies) are fit on the 12-month training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test. This prevents future information from leaking into training. No feature directly encodes the label or uses post-incident artifacts.

### 5. Model and Baseline
The graph model is a temporal graph neural network: it constructs dynamic interaction graphs from sliding 7-day windows, uses temporal graph convolution (e.g., EvolveGCN or temporal attention) to propagate information over daily snapshots, and outputs per-host incident probabilities via a 2-layer MLP. The model is trained with binary cross-entropy loss and class-weight balancing to handle extreme imbalance. The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on the same tabular feature set with identical class-weight balancing. Both models receive equal hyperparameter tuning effort: grid search or Bayesian optimization on the validation set (months 13–15) using the primary metric. The baseline is fair because it uses the same input information and tuning rigor; it represents the current state-of-practice and establishes whether graph structure adds value.

### 6. Primary and Secondary Metrics
The primary metric is incident-level AUC: for each confirmed incident in the test set, the model's predicted probability for the affected host(s) is extracted, and AUC is computed across all incidents. This metric is chosen because (a) the operational objective is incident detection and response, not per-record classification; (b) incident-level aggregation re-balances extreme class imbalance and reflects alert prioritization; (c) it is robust to label delay and post-incident noise. Secondary metrics include: per-incident-type AUC (to assess whether the graph model provides special advantage for rare or highly structured attacks like lateral movement), precision at 95% recall (to measure analyst alert burden), median time-to-detection (hours from incident start to first alert), and per-host precision/recall at a fixed threshold (for raw classification performance comparison).

### 7. Model Selection and Test Set Policy
Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 13–15), with the best configuration selected based on validation incident-level AUC. Both models are then trained once on months 1–15 combined and evaluated on the held-out test set (months 16–18). The test set is touched exactly once, after all tuning and training, ensuring it serves as a genuine measure of generalization to future months.

### 8. Confound Controls
To verify that performance gains reflect temporal interaction learning rather than exploitation of asset centrality or device type, three ablations are performed: (1) train a graph model variant without temporal edge information, using only static metadata, and compare test AUC; (2) stratify test performance by host centrality to detect whether gains concentrate on high-centrality hosts; (3) conduct blind expert review and SHAP-based feature importance analysis to confirm that the graph model learns meaningful temporal patterns (e.g., multi-hop communication chains) rather than static asset cues. These analyses clarify the mechanism driving any observed improvement.
difficulty_justification: The corruption subtly disguises leakage by justifying stratification for 'stable' model comparison while masking temporal overlap between splits. Reviewers must recognize that operational validity for sequential prediction tasks requires strict temporal isolation, even if it sacrifices short-term class balance, and connect this to the 7-day label window's compound leakage effects.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that jointly uses SIEM alert metadata, historical analyst disposition, and incident response ticket text will surface truly malicious alerts in the top 10 more effectively than a binary alert classifier trained on the same features, as measured by precision at top-k on chronologically held-out incidents.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Learning-to-Rank vs. Binary Classification for SOC Alert Triage

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 18 months of production SIEM alerts from a single enterprise SOC. All alerts are retained in temporal order with full metadata: rule ID, severity, source/destination entities, timestamps, and any payload context. Ground truth labels are obtained from two sources: (a) analyst dispositions recorded in the SIEM (benign, suspicious, confirmed malicious) where available, and (b) linked incident response tickets where the resolution outcome confirms a security incident. Alerts with no analyst disposition and no ticket link remain unlabeled and are excluded from training and validation but are included in the test set to reflect the operational reality that many alerts are never fully investigated. This preserves class imbalance and missing-label structure rather than artificially balancing or discarding uncertain cases.

### 2. Temporal Split to Prevent Leakage

The 18-month period is divided chronologically: months 1–9 for training, months 10–12 for validation and hyperparameter tuning, and months 13–18 as a locked test set. This temporal split is critical because cybersecurity alert patterns are non-stationary—detection rules are updated, infrastructure changes, and attacker tactics evolve. A random train-test split would allow the model to interpolate between similar alerts occurring at different times, falsely inflating generalization performance. The chronological split forces the model to learn from past patterns and rank new, unseen alert types correctly, which is the true deployment scenario.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Features are constructed from alert metadata and incident ticket text:
- **Alert metadata**: rule ID, severity, alert count from the same source in the past 24 hours, time-of-day and day-of-week, source/destination entity types, and historical disposition rates (computed on training data only to prevent lookahead bias).
- **Temporal features**: days since the last similar alert, cumulative alert volume from the source.
- **Text features**: incident ticket title and description are vectorized using TF-IDF or pre-trained transformer embeddings, aggregated as mean vectors when multiple tickets are linked to an alert.

All preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler for numeric features, one-hot/target encoding for categoricals, TF-IDF vectorizer for text) are fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Missing values are imputed using statistics (mean/mode) learned from training data. This ensures no information from validation or test distributions leaks into preprocessing decisions.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a gradient-boosted ranker (LambdaMART via LightGBM or XGBoost with rank objective) or a neural listwise ranker trained on the same feature set. The ranker learns to score alerts such that malicious alerts are ranked higher than benign ones within incident groups or time windows. At test time, alerts are sorted by score and top-k are evaluated.

The baseline is a logistic regression binary classifier trained on identical features and given equal hyperparameter tuning effort. Both models are trained on labeled alerts only. The baseline represents current practice: threshold-based alert classification followed by ranking by confidence score. By using the same features and tuning effort, the comparison isolates the effect of the ranking formulation and joint optimization objective, not feature richness or implementation effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Operational Alignment

The primary metric is **NDCG@10**, which measures how effectively confirmed malicious alerts are concentrated near the top of the ranked list while still preserving credit for getting the broader ordering right within the analyst review band. This is appropriate for SOC triage because analysts do not treat all positions in the top-10 equally in practice: alerts surfaced at ranks 1–3 are acted on first, while lower-ranked items may be reviewed only if capacity remains. Using NDCG@10 therefore captures both relevance and position sensitivity inside the operational review budget more faithfully than a flat precision measure.

Secondary metrics include Recall at top-10 (R@10) to measure missed true positives, P@5 and P@20 to assess performance across different budgets, and P@10 as a direct count-based check on how many malicious alerts appear inside the review set. An ablation metric measures coverage of confirmed incidents: how many true-positive alerts fall outside the top-10, indicating costly misses.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Policy

Hyperparameter tuning is performed on the validation set (months 10–12) via grid or random search. The model configuration with the highest validation NDCG@10 is selected. The test set (months 13–18) is locked and used only once, after all development is complete. Final results are reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via bootstrap over test incidents, stratified by alert rule category.

### 7. Controls for Known Confounds

**Temporal drift**: Separate analysis compares NDCG@10 over early vs. late portions of the test period. If performance degrades, drift is occurring and is quantified.

**Text feature advantage**: An ablation study trains both ranker and baseline with and without incident ticket text. If the ranker's improvement vanishes without text, the gain is due to feature richness, not the ranking formulation.

**Entity/signature leakage**: Train-test overlap in source IPs, rule IDs, and entity tuples is audited. Performance is re-evaluated on test alerts with repeated entities held out; degradation indicates leakage.

**Label noise and class imbalance**: Sensitive analysis trains on high-confidence labels only (confirmed incidents) vs. all labels. Both models use class-weighted training to prevent majority-class bias.

**Stratification checks**: Test performance is reported separately by alert rule category and entity type to ensure the model generalizes across alert types, not just the most common ones.

### 8. Scope and Claims

The experiment establishes that the ranking model surfaces malicious alerts near the top of the triage queue more effectively than binary classification under this specific SOC's temporal distribution and label structure. It does not establish causality for why (whether due to ranking objective, feature access, or model capacity)—ablations address this. Results are specific to the enterprise, rule set, and 18-month window; generalization to other SOCs or time periods requires separate validation.

planted_issues.description: The primary metric (NDCG@10) misaligns with the operational objective. The stated goal is to maximize the count of truly malicious alerts in the fixed analyst review budget (top 10), which requires P@10. NDCG@10 rewards relative ordering within the top-10 but may falsely inflate scores if high-ranking malicious alerts are replaced by lower-ranked ones while maintaining NDCG gains. This weakens the direct link to real-world effectiveness metrics.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split (months 1-9 training, 10-12 validation, 13-18 test) introduces training data scarcity by using less than half the data period for validation and testing.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The allocation reflects real SOC constraints where only 50% of the data (months 10-18) represents the target deployment period. Splitting to allocate more validation/test data would reduce training data volume but not improve generalization—models must adapt to new patterns in future data.

must_not_claim.claim: Including unlabeled alerts in the test set introduces evaluator confusion about true-positive counts.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The test set explicitly includes unlabeled alerts to mirror operational reality: many alerts never receive labels because analysts lack capacity to investigate all. The primary metric (P@10) evaluates only labeled alerts, ensuring validity without requiring labelling of the full test set.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies the metric mismatch and defends P@10 as the correct operational alignment. Defender must acknowledge NDCG@10's limitations and agree that P@10 should be the primary metric given the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Argumentation shows that NDCG@10 cannot directly measure the number of true malicious alerts captured under a fixed analyst review budget, which is the central operational requirement.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Defensive rebuttal concedes that while NDCG@10 captures ranking quality nuances, the experiment should prioritize P@10 as the primary metric because the hypothesis explicitly calls for measuring the count of malicious alerts in the top-10, not their distribution quality.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Argumentation demonstrates that P@10 directly measures the binary presence of malicious alerts in the budget-constrained review window, while NDCG@10 allows potentially misleading optimization for order reshuffling.

sound_design_reference: The sound design reference matches the original 'experiment design' section under hypothesis Stage 1, with the primary metric explicitly defined as Precision@10 (P@10).
difficulty_justification: The case requires domain-specific understanding of SOC operations: distinguishing between metrics that measure raw count of actionable true positives (P@10) versus those capturing ordering quality (NDCG@10), even though both are technically valid ranking metrics. The flaw appears plausible under surface-level ML methodology but fails alignment with the hypothesis’s precise operational constraints.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection, a sequence model trained on ordered network flow and endpoint telemetry over the previous 24 hours will detect novel insider-threat or lateral-movement incidents with higher AUROC and earlier alert lead time than a tabular model using only aggregated daily counts and static asset/user metadata.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection using network flow logs and endpoint/network telemetry from a large corporate environment
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence vs. Tabular Models for Enterprise Intrusion Detection

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

This experiment will use 18–24 months of time-stamped network flow logs, endpoint telemetry (process execution, file access, network connections), VPN/SSO authentication logs, and SOC incident tickets from a single large enterprise or consolidated multi-tenant environment with organizational isolation enforced. The raw event granularity (individual flows and endpoint events) must be preserved; pre-aggregated data is not acceptable because the sequence model depends on temporal ordering.

Labels are derived from confirmed security incidents documented in the SOC ticketing system and validated by post-incident investigations. An incident is marked positive if it involved confirmed insider threat activity, lateral movement, or data exfiltration. The label is assigned at the incident start time—the earliest event in the attack sequence identified during the investigation. Incidents with ambiguous classification or insufficient evidence are excluded to maintain label quality. A random sample of 50–100 incidents from across the data period will be independently reviewed by two security analysts; disagreement rates and consensus resolution will be documented to surface label consistency issues.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

To respect temporal dependencies and prevent future information leakage, the data is split chronologically into three contiguous windows:
- **Training set:** Months 1–12
- **Validation set:** Months 13–15
- **Test set:** Months 16–18

This temporal split is critical because (a) attack sequences unfold over time and events from month 18 cannot influence model training on month 1 data, and (b) it mirrors the real deployment scenario where the model is trained on historical data and must detect novel incidents going forward. No incident, user, or asset is split across these windows.

Additionally, a secondary hold-out test cohort is constructed by selecting confirmed incidents from months 16–18 that involve users and assets not encountered during training. This held-out cohort tests whether the sequence model generalizes to novel entities or whether its gains are driven by overfitting to the training population.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**For the sequence model:** Construct time-ordered sequences of network flow and endpoint events over a sliding 24-hour lookback window for each user or asset. Each event in the sequence includes: source/destination IP, port, protocol, flow volume (bytes and packets), flow duration, process name, user, domain, parent process, command-line argument hash, file path hash, and event type (read, write, execute, network connection). Events are timestamped and event-type is encoded as categorical. Sequences are padded or truncated to a fixed length (e.g., the 95th percentile of sequence length) to enable batching.

**For the tabular baseline:** Construct daily aggregated features: total inbound/outbound flow counts, unique destination IPs per day, protocol distribution, process diversity (entropy of process names), count of failed authentications, VPN connection events, and failed login attempts. Include static metadata: user role, asset criticality level, department, asset operating system, user tenure at the organization, historical incident involvement (whether the user or asset has been involved in prior confirmed incidents), and a rolling 24-hour analyst-response activity count derived from SOC workflow systems, such as ticket touches, triage updates, and enrichment actions linked to the user or asset. This operational signal is retained because it is already available in the enterprise telemetry stack and can help separate ordinary bursts of system activity from cases that are accumulating broader investigative context.

**Both models receive the same underlying telemetry and operational context; they differ only in temporal resolution and representation.** All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. No statistics or parameters from validation or test data inform preprocessing.

### 4. Model and Baseline Specification

**Sequence model:** A bidirectional LSTM or Transformer encoder with multi-head self-attention. The encoder accepts variable-length event sequences and outputs a fixed-dimension representation. This representation is passed through a 2-layer dense classifier with dropout. The model is trained to minimize binary cross-entropy loss with class weights to account for imbalance (weight for positive class = fraction of negative examples / fraction of positive examples). Hyperparameters to tune: LSTM hidden size (or Transformer hidden dimension), number of attention heads, dropout rate, L2 regularization, learning rate, and batch size. Early stopping is applied using validation AUROC.

**Baseline:** Gradient boosted trees (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on the daily aggregated and static features. The baseline is given equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters (tree depth, learning rate, L1/L2 regularization, subsample fraction) are optimized using the same budget as the sequence model (e.g., 100 random search trials). The baseline is evaluated under identical conditions (same splits, same metrics, same threshold selection).

This design ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of sequential modeling, not differential hyperparameter effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Validation Approach

**Primary metric: AUROC** (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve). AUROC is chosen because (1) it is insensitive to severe class imbalance, (2) it aggregates performance across all classification thresholds, enabling the SOC team to select their preferred sensitivity–specificity trade-off, and (3) it directly supports the stakeholder objective of maximizing the fraction of true attacks detected.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **Alert lead time:** For incidents detected by both models, measure the difference in hours between when the sequence model first alerts and when the baseline first alerts. The sequence model should alert earlier.
- **Precision at fixed recall:** Precision@90% Recall and Precision@95% Recall, showing false-positive rates at operationally relevant sensitivity levels.
- **AUPR** (Area Under Precision-Recall Curve), which emphasizes performance in the positive class tail.
- **Time-to-detection:** Median and 95th percentile hours from confirmed incident start time to model alert, stratified by incident type (insider threat vs. lateral movement).

**Model selection and hyperparameter tuning:** Both the sequence model and baseline are trained on the training set (months 1–12). At each candidate hyperparameter configuration, the model is evaluated on the validation set (months 13–15) using AUROC. The configuration with the highest validation AUROC is selected and locked in. After validation-based selection is complete, a single pre-deployment verification pass is run on the months 16–18 hold-out to confirm that the chosen operating configuration remains stable in the most recent environment before the final report is frozen. This pass is limited to deployment-facing decisions that should not materially alter ranking performance, such as fixing the alert threshold used for lead-time comparisons and, if necessary, selecting between the top two validation-performing configurations when their validation AUROC is effectively tied but their probability calibration differs. Final performance metrics are then reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via stratified bootstrap resampling.

### 6. Hypothesis Testing and Scope

The hypothesis is supported if the sequence model's test AUROC exceeds the baseline's test AUROC by a pre-specified margin (e.g., ≥0.03) and if lead time is meaningfully shorter. If this threshold is not met, the hypothesis is not supported.

**Confound controls:** To rule out incident-response artifacts (e.g., the sequence model exploiting patterns in how incidents are investigated rather than detecting true anomalies), performance is additionally evaluated on the hold-out cohort of incidents involving previously unseen users and assets. If the advantage persists, this suggests the model is capturing behavioral signal. To mitigate label noise, incidents with low analyst confidence are excluded, and a 10% random sample of labels is validated by a second analyst. To detect distribution shift, the distribution of event types, user/asset populations, and attack categories across train, validation, and test windows is compared; any significant shift is noted and results are qualified accordingly.

**What the experiment establishes:** Whether a sequence model, given access to 24-hour ordered event sequences, outperforms a tabular model on this organization's data and incidents. **What it does not establish:** Whether the sequence model will generalize to new organizational environments, different infrastructure, or fundamentally new attack tactics not represented in the training data. Transfer learning and domain adaptation are separate questions requiring additional validation on out-of-distribution incidents.

planted_issues.description: The tabular baseline includes a rolling 24-hour analyst-response activity count derived from SOC workflow systems (ticket touches, triage updates). Since labels are anchored at the incident start time and analyst activity could occur within this window, this introduces label leakage. The feature gives the model access to post-label information about analyst behavior triggered by the incident, creating a correlation not present in operational settings.

The test set policy allows a verification pass on the hold-out test data (months 16–18) for deployment decisions like threshold selection. This violates the principle that test data must remain untouched during model development. Even limited post-validation selection based on test performance risks overfitting, especially for metrics like alert lead time that are part of the claimed advantage.

must_not_claim.claim: The secondary hold-out test set with novel user/asset identities is unnecessary for confound control
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The secondary hold-out cohort is a legitimate design choice to validate generalization to new entities and ensure sequence model improvements stem from behavioral signal rather than memorization of training population artifacts.

must_not_claim.claim: Temporal splitting into consecutive monthly windows creates an unrealistic evaluation scenario
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting with chronological windows directly mirrors operational deployment where models are trained on historical data and detect incidents in future data, making this a valid design decision for cybersecurity workloads.

acceptable_resolutions.description: If the target leakage is identified and the analyst-response feature is removed from the tabular baseline, the comparison between models becomes fair. This resolves the primary flaw compromising validity.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that the analyst-response feature encodes information about incident labels through post-incident workflow actions, and that this feature improves baseline model performance when included.

acceptable_resolutions.description: If the test set verification pass is eliminated and evaluation strictly follows the original validation-selected hyperparameters, the contamination issue is resolved and final metrics represent unbiased performance estimation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that the verification pass on test data influences threshold selection or model configuration choices that materially affect final lead time measurements or AUROC rankings.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: If the defender demonstrates that the verification pass only affects deployment decisions without altering model architecture or fundamental ranking behavior, this partial resolution would partially mitigate but not fully resolve the contamination concern.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence vs. Tabular Models for Enterprise Intrusion Detection

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

This experiment will use 18–24 months of time-stamped network flow logs, endpoint telemetry (process execution, file access, network connections), VPN/SSO authentication logs, and SOC incident tickets from a single large enterprise or consolidated multi-tenant environment with organizational isolation enforced. The raw event granularity (individual flows and endpoint events) must be preserved; pre-aggregated data is not acceptable because the sequence model depends on temporal ordering.

Labels are derived from confirmed security incidents documented in the SOC ticketing system and validated by post-incident investigations. An incident is marked positive if it involved confirmed insider threat activity, lateral movement, or data exfiltration. The label is assigned at the incident start time—the earliest event in the attack sequence identified during the investigation. Incidents with ambiguous classification or insufficient evidence are excluded to maintain label quality. A random sample of 50–100 incidents from across the data period will be independently reviewed by two security analysts; disagreement rates and consensus resolution will be documented to surface label consistency issues.

### 2. Data Splitting Strategy

To respect temporal dependencies and prevent future information leakage, the data is split chronologically into three contiguous windows:
- **Training set:** Months 1–12
- **Validation set:** Months 13–15
- **Test set:** Months 16–18

This temporal split is critical because (a) attack sequences unfold over time and events from month 18 cannot influence model training on month 1 data, and (b) it mirrors the real deployment scenario where the model is trained on historical data and must detect novel incidents going forward. No incident, user, or asset is split across these windows.

Additionally, a secondary hold-out test cohort is constructed by selecting confirmed incidents from months 16–18 that involve users and assets not encountered during training. This held-out cohort tests whether the sequence model generalizes to novel entities or whether its gains are driven by overfitting to the training population.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**For the sequence model:** Construct time-ordered sequences of network flow and endpoint events over a sliding 24-hour lookback window for each user or asset. Each event in the sequence includes: source/destination IP, port, protocol, flow volume (bytes and packets), flow duration, process name, user, domain, parent process, command-line argument hash, file path hash, and event type (read, write, execute, network connection). Events are timestamped and event-type is encoded as categorical. Sequences are padded or truncated to a fixed length (e.g., the 95th percentile of sequence length) to enable batching.

**For the tabular baseline:** Construct daily aggregated features: total inbound/outbound flow counts, unique destination IPs per day, protocol distribution, process diversity (entropy of process names), count of failed authentications, VPN connection events, and failed login attempts. Include static metadata: user role, asset criticality level, department, asset operating system, user tenure at the organization, and historical incident involvement (whether the user or asset has been involved in prior confirmed incidents).

**Both models receive the same underlying telemetry; they differ only in temporal resolution and representation.** All preprocessing (scaling, encoding, imputation) is fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. No statistics or parameters from validation or test data inform preprocessing.

### 4. Model and Baseline Specification

**Sequence model:** A bidirectional LSTM or Transformer encoder with multi-head self-attention. The encoder accepts variable-length event sequences and outputs a fixed-dimension representation. This representation is passed through a 2-layer dense classifier with dropout. The model is trained to minimize binary cross-entropy loss with class weights to account for imbalance (weight for positive class = fraction of negative examples / fraction of positive examples). Hyperparameters to tune: LSTM hidden size (or Transformer hidden dimension), number of attention heads, dropout rate, L2 regularization, learning rate, and batch size. Early stopping is applied using validation AUROC.

**Baseline:** Gradient boosted trees (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on the daily aggregated and static features. The baseline is given equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters (tree depth, learning rate, L1/L2 regularization, subsample fraction) are optimized using the same budget as the sequence model (e.g., 100 random search trials). The baseline is evaluated under identical conditions (same splits, same metrics, same threshold selection).

This design ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of sequential modeling, not differential hyperparameter effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Validation Approach

**Primary metric: AUROC** (Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve). AUROC is chosen because (1) it is insensitive to severe class imbalance, (2) it aggregates performance across all classification thresholds, enabling the SOC team to select their preferred sensitivity–specificity trade-off, and (3) it directly supports the stakeholder objective of maximizing the fraction of true attacks detected.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **Alert lead time:** For incidents detected by both models, measure the difference in hours between when the sequence model first alerts and when the baseline first alerts. The sequence model should alert earlier.
- **Precision at fixed recall:** Precision@90% Recall and Precision@95% Recall, showing false-positive rates at operationally relevant sensitivity levels.
- **AUPR** (Area Under Precision-Recall Curve), which emphasizes performance in the positive class tail.
- **Time-to-detection:** Median and 95th percentile hours from confirmed incident start time to model alert, stratified by incident type (insider threat vs. lateral movement).

**Model selection and hyperparameter tuning:** Both the sequence model and baseline are trained on the training set (months 1–12). At each candidate hyperparameter configuration, the model is evaluated on the validation set (months 13–15) using AUROC. The configuration with the highest validation AUROC is selected and locked in. **The test set (months 16–18) is held aside and used exactly once, at the end, to report final performance metrics.** Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals computed via stratified bootstrap resampling.

### 6. Hypothesis Testing and Scope

The hypothesis is supported if the sequence model's test AUROC exceeds the baseline's test AUROC by a pre-specified margin (e.g., ≥0.03) and if lead time is meaningfully shorter. If this threshold is not met, the hypothesis is not supported.

**Confound controls:** To rule out incident-response artifacts (e.g., the sequence model exploiting patterns in how incidents are investigated rather than detecting true anomalies), performance is additionally evaluated on the hold-out cohort of incidents involving previously unseen users and assets. If the advantage persists, this suggests the model is capturing behavioral signal. To mitigate label noise, incidents with low analyst confidence are excluded, and a 10% random sample of labels is validated by a second analyst. To detect distribution shift, the distribution of event types, user/asset populations, and attack categories across train, validation, and test windows is compared; any significant shift is noted and results are qualified accordingly.

**What the experiment establishes:** Whether a sequence model, given access to 24-hour ordered event sequences, outperforms a tabular model on this organization's data and incidents. **What it does not establish:** Whether the sequence model will generalize to new organizational environments, different infrastructure, or fundamentally new attack tactics not represented in the training data. Transfer learning and domain adaptation are separate questions requiring additional validation on out-of-distribution incidents.
difficulty_justification: The corruptions are challenging because the leakage arises from an operationally plausible feature (analyst-response metrics) that appears domain-relevant, and test set contamination manifests as a seemingly justified quality control step rather than overt leakage—both require careful temporal reasoning to detect.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection, a sequence model trained on ordered network flow and endpoint telemetry over the previous 24 hours will detect malicious activity better than a tabular baseline built from aggregate daily summaries, as measured by precision-recall AUC on future-day incidents.
domain: Enterprise cybersecurity intrusion detection using network flow logs and endpoint/network telemetry from a large corporate environment
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Models vs. Tabular Baselines for Intrusion Detection

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses 18 months of production security data from the enterprise environment, including timestamped events (process launches, DNS queries, authentication attempts, network connections, file/email activity) from SIEM, EDR, and network flow logs. Each day is labeled by incident response teams as either confirmed malicious (1) or benign (0), based on analyst consensus review. A label is considered valid only if it arrives within 7 days of the incident close or initial detection; this window ensures label quality while maintaining operational realism. Incidents with contradictory or delayed labels (>7 days) are excluded. All events within the 24 hours preceding the label are retained as the feature observation window.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To prevent information leakage and reflect the deployment scenario, the data is split chronologically—not randomly. The training set comprises months 1–12 (12 months of historical data), the validation set comprises months 13–15 (3 months for hyperparameter tuning and model selection), and the held-out test set comprises months 16–18 (3 months of future, unseen data). This temporal ordering ensures that no future information (events or labels from later months) influences model training, and the test set represents realistic future performance where attacker behavior may drift.

### 3. Feature Construction and Preprocessing

For the sequence model, raw features are extracted as ordered event sequences over each 24-hour window: event type, timestamp, source/destination identifiers, process/domain names, success/failure flags, and anomaly scores. Events are sorted chronologically, embedded into learned representations, and padded or truncated to a fixed sequence length (e.g., max 500 events). For the tabular baseline, the same 24-hour window is aggregated into daily summaries: event counts by type, counts of unique IPs/users/processes, counts of failed authentications, binary flags for rare events, and max/min/mean anomaly scores. All preprocessing (scaling, embedding vocabularies, one-hot encoding) is parameterized once using corpus-level statistics from the full 18-month log inventory before model fitting, and those parameters are then frozen and applied identically across train, validation, and test windows. Using a single preprocessing parameterization avoids month-specific shifts in identifier frequency and anomaly-score scale from being mistaken for model improvements, which is especially important when comparing sequence and tabular representations built from the same underlying telemetry.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The sequence model is an LSTM or Transformer encoder with 2–3 layers (128–256 hidden units), followed by global pooling and a 2-layer MLP with ReLU activation and dropout (0.3), ending in a sigmoid output for binary classification. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss with class weights to account for imbalance. The baseline is logistic regression with L2 regularization, trained on the same aggregate features and label set. To preserve its role as a stable operational reference, logistic regression uses a fixed regularization setting rather than an extended hyperparameter sweep; model-development effort is concentrated on the sequence architecture, where capacity and optimization choices materially affect temporal pattern learning. The baseline still receives the same preprocessing and validation split, and its predicted probabilities are evaluated under the same metric stack, providing a lightweight but practical comparison point.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Precision-Recall AUC**, chosen because (a) the task has severe class imbalance (confirmed incidents are 0.1–1% of daily observations), and PR-AUC is insensitive to true negatives and focuses on ranking quality on the rare positive class; (b) security teams operationally care about surfacing true incidents while controlling alert fatigue—PR-AUC reflects this ranking quality; (c) analysts can set a threshold on the model's predicted probabilities to control false-positive rates. Secondary metrics include recall at fixed false-positive rates (e.g., 1%, 5%), precision at fixed recall levels (e.g., 80%), Brier score for calibration assessment, and per-incident-type confusion matrices to identify failure modes.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters (learning rate, regularization strength, early-stopping patience, sequence model architecture size) are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using PR-AUC as the selection criterion. Early stopping is applied: during training on months 1–12, the model state achieving the best validation PR-AUC is retained. The test set is never consulted during model development. After model selection is complete, a single forward pass is performed on the held-out test set (months 16–18), and metrics are computed with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To isolate the contribution of temporal sequence modeling: (1) both models observe identical 24-hour windows and use identical labels, eliminating window-size and label-quality confounds; (2) a secondary analysis trains a sequence model on aggregate-only features to disentangle whether improvements come from temporal ordering or feature richness; (3) performance is reported per month (months 16, 17, 18) to assess generalization to attacker behavior drift; (4) all temporal statistics (e.g., month-level averages) are excluded from training. The experiment establishes that sequence models rank intrusions better than tabular aggregates on future data. It does not establish which architectural components (LSTM vs. Transformer) or feature subsets are optimal, nor does it prove causality of any single design choice—these require follow-up ablations.

planted_issues.description: Preprocessing uses statistics from the full 18-month corpus, including future data, which illegally exposes the model to validation/test statistics. This creates an unrealistic best-case scenario for the baseline and sequence model, inflating performance estimates. In security settings where attacker behavior drifts monthly, this prevents proper evaluation of generalization to unseen adversarial patterns.

The tabular baseline is deliberately under-tuned (uses fixed regularization) while the sequence model receives extensive hyperparameter optimization. This skews the comparison by testing the sequence model's best-case performance against the baseline's minimum-viable effort, eliminating the possibility of distinguishing architectural superiority from search effort differences.

must_not_claim.claim: Critics might argue that the 12–3–3 temporal split is unbalanced and undermines validation stability
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The split structure mirrors real deployment conditions where models predict forward in time. Stability is preserved by using a validation window (months 13–15) with sufficient duration to capture attacker behavior variations and seasonality effects.

must_not_claim.claim: PR-AUC is an inappropriate metric because it ignores true negatives in binary classification
must_not_claim.why_wrong: In intrusion detection with rare positives (0.1–1%), PR-AUC is ideal because security teams prioritize detection quality over absolute classification rates. The metric aligns with real-world alert ranking requirements and provides interpretable thresholds for operational alert budgeting.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic demonstrates that corpus-level preprocessing leaks future data into training statistics, invalidating the generalization assessment. Defense agrees that only train-only parameterization ensures valid evaluation of temporal drift robustness.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Demonstration that month-specific identifier distributions (e.g., IP anomaly scores) change significantly over 18 months, making corpus-aggregates unsuitable for frozen preprocessing

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic shows the baseline receives unequal tuning effort, exaggerating sequence model benefits. Defense admits that both models must undergo equivalent hyperparameter selection for a valid comparison.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Quantitative analysis confirming regularization sweep improves baseline AUC by >1% (or demonstrates meaningful variation across grid values), indicating omitted comparison cost

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

Each day is labeled by incident response teams as either confirmed malicious (1) or benign (0), based on analyst consensus review. Incidents with contradictory or delayed labels (>7 days) are excluded. All events within the 24 hours preceding the label are retained as the feature observation window.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The training set comprises months 1–12 (12 months of historical data), the validation set comprises months 13–15 (3 months for hyperparameter tuning and model selection), and the held-out test set comprises months 16–18 (3 months of future, unseen data). This temporal ordering ensures that no future information (events or labels from later months) influences model training, and the test set represents realistic future performance where attacker behavior may drift.

### 3. Feature Construction and Preprocessing

Events are sorted chronologically, embedded into learned representations, and padded or truncated to a fixed sequence length (e.g., max 500 events). For the tabular baseline, the same 24-hour window is aggregated into daily summaries: event counts by type, counts of unique IPs/users/processes, counts of failed authentications, binary flags for rare events, and max/min/mean anomaly scores. All preprocessing (scaling, embedding, one-hot encoding) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets. This prevents information leakage from future months into the training procedure.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The sequence model is an LSTM or Transformer encoder with 2–3 layers (128–256 hidden units), followed by global pooling and a 2-layer MLP with ReLU activation and dropout (0.3), ending in a sigmoid output for binary classification. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss with class weights to account for imbalance. The baseline is logistic regression with L2 regularization, trained on the same aggregate features and label set. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters are tuned via grid search on the validation set, and early stopping is applied during training using validation PR-AUC. The logistic regression baseline is fair: it uses standard, practical methods, receives the same preprocessing and validation discipline, and represents a reasonable operational comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **Precision-Recall AUC**, chosen because (a) the task has severe class imbalance (confirmed incidents are 0.1–1% of daily observations), and PR-AUC is insensitive to true negatives and focuses on ranking quality on the rare positive class; (b) security teams operationally care about surfacing true incidents while controlling alert fatigue—PR-AUC reflects this ranking quality; (c) analysts can set a threshold on the model's predicted probabilities to control false-positive rates. Secondary metrics include recall at fixed false-positive rates (e.g., 1%, 5%), precision at fixed recall levels (e.g., 80%), Brier score for calibration assessment, and per-incident-type confusion matrices to identify failure modes.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters (learning rate, regularization strength, early-stopping patience, sequence model architecture size) are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using PR-AUC as the selection criterion. Early stopping is applied: during training on months 1–12, the model state achieving the best validation PR-AUC is retained. The test set is never consulted during model development. After model selection is complete, a single forward pass is performed on the held-out test set (months 16–18), and metrics are computed with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To isolate the contribution of temporal sequence modeling: (1) both models observe identical 24-hour windows and use identical labels, eliminating window-size and label-quality confounds; (2) a secondary analysis trains a sequence model on aggregate-only features to disentangle whether improvements come from temporal ordering or feature richness; (3) performance is reported per month (months 16, 17, 18) to assess generalization to attacker behavior drift; (4) all temporal statistics (e.g., month-level averages) are excluded from training. The experiment establishes that sequence models rank intrusions better than tabular aggregates on future data. It does not establish which architectural components (LSTM vs. Transformer) or feature subsets are optimal, nor does it prove causality of any single design choice—these require follow-up ablations.
difficulty_justification: This case combines subtle preprocessing leakage with an imbalanced model comparison strategy. The preprocessing contamination requires domain knowledge to detect (real-world security models must handle temporal drift) while the baseline mismatch tests understanding of fair model evaluation principles in resource-constrained operational contexts.

hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network that models user-device-session relationships and message propagation patterns will detect enterprise identity-compromise attacks better than a feature-engineered gradient-boosted baseline, as measured by alert-level precision at a fixed low false-positive rate.
domain: Cybersecurity — enterprise identity and access monitoring from endpoint, VPN, and SSO logs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Graph Neural Networks for Enterprise Identity-Compromise Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 18–24 months of production authentication events from a large enterprise's SSO, VPN, endpoint, and directory services. Raw signals include: timestamp, user ID, device ID, device fingerprint, source IP, geolocation, MFA outcome, session duration, and user metadata (role, department). Labels are derived from confirmed incident-response cases: a user-device-session event is marked positive if the account was confirmed compromised and the event predates discovery by at least 24 hours (ensuring the event could have informed early detection). Events not linked to confirmed compromises are marked negative. Unconfirmed or weakly-labeled events (analyst hunts, suspected but unverified incidents) are held out and used only for post-hoc sensitivity checks, preventing hindsight bias from inflating training performance.

### 2. Split Strategy and Temporal Alignment

Data is split chronologically: training (months 1–12), validation (months 13–15), test (months 16–18). This split is essential because authentication patterns exhibit strong temporal dependence—attack tactics evolve, users change behavior seasonally, and deployment requires the model to predict on live, unseen future data. Within each split, events are stratified by user risk tier (pre-computed based on organizational role and asset criticality) to ensure each cohort is represented in training, validation, and test. This prevents the model from learning spurious associations between user criticality and compromise rate.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Preprocessing parameters are established once from the full corpus of available authentication traffic before model fitting so that geolocation clusters, rare device-fingerprint buckets, and scaling constants reflect the enterprise-wide support of the feature space rather than a single historical window. Those parameters are then frozen and applied uniformly during training, validation, and test evaluation to keep the representation consistent across all periods. The tGNN ingests temporal graph features: user ego-network properties (degree, clustering coefficient), aggregated neighbor states (peer session durations, login success rates), recency indicators (time since last login), sequence statistics (time deltas between consecutive events, MFA failure streaks), static metadata (role, device type), and analyst-enriched security context such as whether the user-device pair enters an elevated-review queue within the next 24 hours, which serves as a compact proxy for cross-sensor risk already available in the investigation pipeline. The baseline receives hand-engineered temporal features with equivalent coverage: login velocity (logins per day over 7- and 30-day windows), device novelty (days since first seen, device count per user), geolocation anomaly (distance from usual location cluster), peer aggregates (median/max velocity among department peers), time-of-day indicators, and recency features. Both models receive the same raw signals; the difference lies in how they combine them.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Neural Network (e.g., EvolveGCN or DyRep-style architecture) that embeds users, devices, and sessions as nodes in a directed graph. Edges represent authentication events with edge features (timestamp, MFA outcome, duration). A temporal convolution layer propagates information forward in time, then predicts a binary logit for each user-device-session event. The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost/LightGBM) with hand-engineered temporal and network features. Both models receive identical preprocessing, validation-set hyperparameter tuning (Bayesian search, 100+ configurations, equal wall-clock time), and the same data splits. This ensures any difference in performance reflects the benefit of learned graph structure, not disparities in tuning effort or model capacity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Operational Alignment

The primary metric will be **AUROC computed at the event level** over the held-out period. This preserves the model's full ranking over the authentication stream and avoids tying model comparison to a single operating point that may vary with month-to-month traffic mix, analyst staffing, or campaign intensity. Because the hypothesis is that temporal graph structure improves separation between compromised and benign events, a global ranking metric provides the cleanest test of whether the model learns a stronger signal before scores are translated into any specific alert threshold. Secondary metrics include recall at FPR=2%, the precision-recall curve, and Expected Calibration Error (are predicted scores interpretable as probabilities?). These additional views allow inspection of low-FPR behavior and score reliability once the ranking comparison is established.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for both the tGNN and baseline are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using the same primary metric (event-level AUROC). The configuration achieving the highest validation performance is provisionally locked in. After validation-based hyperparameter selection, the held-out test period will be used for a final stability check to confirm that the chosen configuration preserves its relative advantage under the most recent traffic conditions before the model is retrained on the combined training and validation windows for the definitive comparison. This step is intended to filter out configurations whose validation gains are specific to the middle time window rather than robust to later-period drift. Final test-set reporting will include 95% confidence intervals bootstrapped across stratified samples.

### 7. Controls for Confounds and Scope

The baseline is given rich recency and sequence features—login velocity, time deltas, MFA failure streaks—ensuring that if the tGNN outperforms, the gain is not merely from access to temporal information but from learned graph propagation. Concept drift is monitored by evaluating both models independently on each test month; if the tGNN's advantage erodes over time, the learned structure is brittle. Performance is also stratified by user risk tier, ensuring the result is not driven by a single cohort. Post-hoc evaluation includes a secondary manually-reviewed incident set to guard against partial-label bias in training.

The experiment establishes whether temporal graph architectures rank identity-compromise events more effectively than engineered temporal baselines on enterprise authentication data. It does *not* establish deployment success, real-world response time, or operational security outcomes—those require production A/B testing.

planted_issues.description: The addition of 'analyst-enriched security context' in features introduces target leakage. This feature depends on future analyst workflows that occur after the event's timestamp and closely correlate with compromise labels, invalidating the model's ability to detect attacks in real time.

Using the test set for configuration stability checks before retraining introduces evaluation contamination. This invalidates generalization estimates by allowing the test set to influence model selection, biasing results in favor of configurations that perform well on this specific data slice.

Preprocessing parameters derived from the full data corpus leak distributional information from validation/test periods into training. This creates artificial patterns unknown at deployment time, leading to over-optimistic performance estimates in a domain with evolving user behavior and attack tactics.

Using AUROC as the primary metric misaligns evaluation with the SOC's operational constraint of strict alert-budget enforcement (2% FPR). AUROC can show strong separation quality while precision in the critical low-FPR regime remains unacceptably poor.

must_not_claim.claim: Stratifying by user risk tier during chronological splitting creates imbalance and overfits to specific user cohorts
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal stratified splitting by precomputed risk tiers is a legitimate design choice to prevent spurious associations between criticality and compromise rate while maintaining temporal structure—essential for domains with evolving patterns.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline lacks recency and sequence features, making any difference attributable to richer temporal modeling rather than graph structure
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline explicitly includes login velocity, time deltas, and MFA streak features to ensure its comparison isolates graph-propagation benefits, a known critical confound in this domain.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates that analyst-enriched features in the feature set exhibit strong statistical dependence on labels. The verdict concludes that target leakage invalidates the experiment's ability to detect real compromises due to future data availability constraints.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Feature ablation studies confirm the removed feature strongly correlates with the label, or temporal analysis shows its post-event derivation invalidates model generalization.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic proves that preprocessing leakage enables the model to exploit distributional artifacts from future periods. The verdict concludes performance gains are illusory and would fail under true deployment conditions without these patterns.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Comparative evaluation using proper preprocessing shows significant performance degradation, proving overfitting to future data structure.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic shows AUROC-optimized models perform poorly under the required 2% FPR precision threshold. The verdict concludes the design's evaluation methodology failed to test the hypothesis under operational constraints.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Empirical evidence that top AUROC configurations rank true positives poorly in the low-FPR regime critical to analyst capacity limitations.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Temporal Graph Neural Networks for Enterprise Identity-Compromise Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 18–24 months of production authentication events from a large enterprise's SSO, VPN, endpoint, and directory services. Raw signals include: timestamp, user ID, device ID, device fingerprint, source IP, geolocation, MFA outcome, session duration, and user metadata (role, department). Labels are derived from confirmed incident-response cases: a user-device-session event is marked positive if the account was confirmed compromised and the event predates discovery by at least 24 hours (ensuring the event could have informed early detection). Events not linked to confirmed compromises are marked negative. Unconfirmed or weakly-labeled events (analyst hunts, suspected but unverified incidents) are held out and used only for post-hoc sensitivity checks, preventing hindsight bias from inflating training performance.

### 2. Split Strategy and Temporal Alignment

Data is split chronologically, not randomly: training (months 1–12), validation (months 13–15), test (months 16–18). This split is essential because authentication patterns exhibit strong temporal dependence—attack tactics evolve, users change behavior seasonally, and deployment requires the model to predict on live, unseen future data. Random splitting would leak future events into training and artificially inflate performance. Within each split, events are stratified by user risk tier (pre-computed based on organizational role and asset criticality) to ensure each cohort is represented in training, validation, and test. This prevents the model from learning spurious associations between user criticality and compromise rate.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing transformers (IP geolocation clustering, device fingerprint hashing, role encoding, feature scaling) are fit exclusively on the training set. Fit statistics and vocabularies are frozen and applied identically to validation and test sets, preventing leakage. Features are constructed at inference time *before* the target event to avoid encoding label information or future outcomes. The tGNN ingests temporal graph features: user ego-network properties (degree, clustering coefficient), aggregated neighbor states (peer session durations, login success rates), recency indicators (time since last login), sequence statistics (time deltas between consecutive events, MFA failure streaks), and static metadata (role, device type). The baseline receives hand-engineered temporal features with equivalent coverage: login velocity (logins per day over 7- and 30-day windows), device novelty (days since first seen, device count per user), geolocation anomaly (distance from usual location cluster), peer aggregates (median/max velocity among department peers), time-of-day indicators, and recency features. Both models receive the same raw signals; the difference lies in how they combine them.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a Temporal Graph Neural Network (e.g., EvolveGCN or DyRep-style architecture) that embeds users, devices, and sessions as nodes in a directed graph. Edges represent authentication events with edge features (timestamp, MFA outcome, duration). A temporal convolution layer propagates information forward in time, then predicts a binary logit for each user-device-session event. The baseline is a gradient-boosted tree (XGBoost/LightGBM) with hand-engineered temporal and network features. Both models receive identical preprocessing, validation-set hyperparameter tuning (Bayesian search, 100+ configurations, equal wall-clock time), and the same data splits. This ensures any difference in performance reflects the benefit of learned graph structure, not disparities in tuning effort or model capacity.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics and Operational Alignment

The primary metric is **precision at a fixed false-positive rate (FPR) of 2%**. Operationally, the SOC can sustain approximately 2 false alarms per 100 authentication events before analyst fatigue becomes untenable. Precision at FPR=2% measures: among the top-scored events the model flags at this alert budget, how many are true compromises? This directly aligns with the stakeholder's goal of catching real incidents while respecting analyst workload. Secondary metrics include recall at FPR=2% (fraction of true compromises caught), AUROC (overall ranking quality), the precision-recall curve (threshold sensitivity), and Expected Calibration Error (are predicted scores interpretable as probabilities?). These secondary metrics protect against threshold gaming and allow evaluation at alternative operating points (e.g., FPR=1%, 5%).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameters for both the tGNN and baseline are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using the same primary metric (precision at FPR=2%). The configuration achieving the highest validation performance is locked in. The test set (months 16–18) is evaluated exactly once, after model selection is complete, and is never used for tuning, threshold selection, or model refinement.

### 7. Controls for Confounds and Scope

The baseline is given rich recency and sequence features—login velocity, time deltas, MFA failure streaks—ensuring that if the tGNN outperforms, the gain is not merely from access to temporal information but from learned graph propagation. Concept drift is monitored by evaluating both models independently on each test month; if the tGNN's advantage erodes over time, the learned structure is brittle. Performance is also stratified by user risk tier, ensuring the result is not driven by a single cohort. Post-hoc evaluation includes a secondary manually-reviewed incident set to guard against partial-label bias in training.

The experiment establishes whether temporal graph architectures rank identity-compromise events more precisely than engineered temporal baselines under realistic alert budgets. It does *not* establish deployment success, real-world response time, or operational security outcomes—those require production A/B testing.
difficulty_justification: This case combines four distinct issues at varying detectability levels and severity. The subtle leakage mechanisms (metric mismatch and target leakage) require domain-specific understanding of cybersecurity operations and temporal pattern evolution, while preprocessing and test contamination problems add moderate-detectability structural flaws that compound to create misleading findings.

hypothesis: For enterprise cybersecurity operations center alert triage, a learning-to-rank model that jointly uses SIEM alert text, entity graph features, and recent incident-response ticket history will rank true high-severity intrusion alerts above benign or low-priority alerts better than a text-only baseline, as measured by nDCG@10 on future alerts from held-out time periods.
domain: Cybersecurity operations center (SOC) alert triage for enterprise network intrusion detection
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Learning-to-Rank for SOC Alert Triage

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 24 months of production SIEM alert logs from the target enterprise SOC. Each alert record includes: timestamp, alert text, enrichment metadata (host, user, IP, rule ID, threat tags), analyst disposition, and linked incident-response ticket IDs. 

Relevance labels are constructed to reflect analyst-actionable truth while respecting temporal causality. Alerts that receive analyst escalation or are linked to an incident-response ticket within 24 hours are labeled high-relevance; alerts closed as false positive or benign by analyst action within 10 business days are labeled low-relevance. Alerts with no documented disposition within 30 days are excluded, acknowledging that partial feedback is inevitable in SOC operations and that speculative relevance judgments would introduce label bias. Defining the positive class around first-day escalation activity keeps the outcome close to the triage decision point the ranker is intended to support, while avoiding unnecessary dependence on slower downstream incident-confirmation workflows.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

Data is split chronologically: months 1–14 (training, 58%), months 15–17 (validation, 25%), months 18–24 (test, 25%). This temporal split is mandatory because SOC data are non-stationary—attacker tactics, infrastructure, and alert distributions evolve over time. A random split would leak future information into training, artificially boosting performance and failing to measure generalization to truly unseen alerts. The temporal boundary between training and validation ensures that the validation set contains alerts the model has never encountered, and likewise for the test set. Stratification by quarter within each fold ensures representative coverage of seasonal patterns.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Three feature groups are constructed from information available at or before alert generation time:

**Alert text**: tokenized and embedded via a frozen pre-trained transformer (e.g., SBERT), yielding 768-dimensional dense vectors. The embedding model is not updated during the experiment to isolate ranking architecture gains.

**Entity graph features**: for each alert, a subgraph is extracted from a static entity store (host–user–IP–rule relationships from the prior 30 days), aggregated via pooling to fixed dimensions. This captures contextual relationships unavailable in alert text alone.

**Ticket history**: for each alert, count and summary statistics of incident-response tickets linked to the same host/user/IP in the prior 14 days are extracted (e.g., ticket count, severity distribution, mean resolution time). These features capture historical risk associated with the entities involved.

All preprocessing transformers (embeddings, categorical encoders, scalers) are fit on the training set only and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. Features are audit-checked to ensure no future information (e.g., ticket-resolution dates or escalation outcomes) leaks into the feature values.

### 4. Model, Baseline, and Evaluation

**Proposed model**: a learning-to-rank model (e.g., LambdaMART or neural listwise ranker) trained on all three feature groups with a ranking loss (softmax cross-entropy). Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set using nDCG@10 as the selection criterion.

**Baseline**: a learning-to-rank model trained on alert text alone, using the same embedding and ranking loss. The baseline receives equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort and is evaluated under identical conditions. This isolates the contribution of entity graph and ticket-history features.

**Primary metric**: nDCG@10. This is chosen because SOC analysts review alerts in priority order with limited capacity (~10 alerts per shift), and nDCG@10 directly measures whether high-relevance alerts rank above noise at the critical top-10 window. It aligns with the operational objective of improving triage efficiency and surfacing alerts most likely to warrant immediate analyst action.

**Secondary metrics**: Mean Reciprocal Rank (position of first true alert), Precision@1/5/10 (analyst interpretability: 'what % of alerts I review are true?'), nDCG@50 (broader ranking quality), and nDCG@10 stratified by alert category and quarter (confound detection).

### 5. Validation and Test Policy

Model selection (hyperparameter tuning) uses the validation set (months 15–17) only. The test set (months 18–24) is locked at the start and used exactly once for final evaluation. No threshold adjustment, model reselection, or any other fitting on the test set is permitted. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals (bootstrap over test-set samples) to quantify uncertainty. Test-set performance is stratified by quarter and alert category to check for consistent improvement and detect distributional confounds.

### 6. Confound Controls

Several controls ensure that observed improvements reflect ranking architecture gains, not confounds:

- **Feature timing audit**: features are constructed using only pre-alert information; future labels are not encoded.
- **Metadata leakage check**: ticket-history features are validated to ensure they do not inadvertently correlate with the label due to label-data construction artifacts.
- **Stratified evaluation**: nDCG@10 is computed separately by rule category, threat class, and time quarter; localized improvements suggest generalization issues.
- **Ablation study**: a model variant dropping ticket-history features confirms that the entity graph contributes independent signal.
- **Temporal distance**: the test set (months 18–24) is sufficiently distant from training (months 1–14) to reflect genuine future drift.
- **Label quality audit**: analyst-disposition consistency is checked; high inconsistency is reported alongside confidence intervals.

The experiment will establish whether multimodal ranking (text + graph + ticket history) improves alert ordering over text-only ranking on future SOC alerts. It will not establish causality of individual features or generalize to other organizations without replication on their data.

planted_issues.description: The label definition uses analyst escalation or incident ticket linkage within 24 hours as the positive class, which measures early analyst workflow decisions rather than confirmed true intrusions. This misaligns with the hypothesis's focus on surfacing verified high-severity intrusion alerts, causing the evaluation to measure prioritization of process metrics (analyst responses) rather than ground-truth incident relevance.

must_not_claim.claim: Alerts excluded due to missing disposition within 30 days represent wasted training data and should be included with imputed labels
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Excluding uninvestigated alerts avoids speculative relevance labels that would introduce bias. The domain requires actionable truth: only alerts with explicit analyst resolution (escalation or dismissal) provide reliable relevance signals.

must_not_claim.claim: Using nDCG@10 instead of AUC or F1-score is inappropriate for this binary relevance ranking task
must_not_claim.why_wrong: nDCG@10 directly measures utility for analyst workflows where only the top 10 alerts per shift can be inspected. Its graded discounting aligns with the objective of surfacing the highest-severity alerts earliest, unlike metrics that treat all ranks equally.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic demonstrates that the label definition does not measure confirmed intrusion incidents but rather early analyst actions (escalation/ticket linkage). A successful defense must prove this scope mismatch has no impact on the hypothesis evaluation, which is impossible because the hypothesis requires measuring true incident prioritization.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that escalations can be influenced by analyst behavior (e.g., overzealous triage rules) and do not guarantee true incidents, while the hypothesis explicitly ties model success to surfacing verified high-severity attacks.

The temporal boundary between training and validation ensures that the validation set contains alerts the model has never encountered, and likewise for the test set.
